descriptionShort: A bold, bright orange-and-yellow shelf fungus that grows in big layered clusters on trees. Hard to miss.
descriptionLong: Chicken of the Woods grows in overlapping fan-shaped shelves directly on wood. The top surface is vivid orange, fading to yellow at the edges. The underside is bright lemon yellow with tiny pores — no gills. Young specimens are soft and juicy; older ones become tough and dry. It can be massive, sometimes weighing several kilograms. Found on both living and dead trees, especially oaks and cherries in Ontario.
keyTraits: Bright orange top, yellow edges
Lemon-yellow pored underside — no gills
Grows in overlapping shelves on wood
Can be very large — sometimes the size of a dinner plate
expertClues: L. sulphureus grows on hardwoods; L. cincinnatus grows at the base of oaks from roots
Texture changes dramatically with age — young is soft, old is chalky
No stem; attached directly to wood
confusionWarnings: Not easily confused when young — the colour is unmistakable
Old dry specimens lose colour and could confuse a beginner
encounterRarity: Patches appear reliably on the same tree year after year once established

images.attribution: (c) Stephen John Davies, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Stephen John Davies

sourceNotes: Common southern Ontario species. Appears reliably summer through fall on hardwoods.

descriptionShort: A huge white ball that can grow to the size of a volleyball or bigger. Grows in fields, parks, and forest edges.
descriptionLong: Giant Puffballs are unmistakable: large, round to oval, pure white on the outside, with smooth leathery skin. When young and fresh, the inside is completely white and firm. As they age the interior yellows, then turns olive-brown as the spores mature. They grow singly or in small groups in grassy areas, meadows, parks, and forest edges. One of the few mushrooms where the identifying feature — totally white solid interior — also means it's safe to confirm what you have.
keyTraits: Large white ball — can be volleyball-sized or bigger
No cap, no stem, no gills — just a white ball
Interior pure white and firm when young
expertClues: A cross-section check is important: interior must be pure white all the way through — no outline of a figure inside (which would indicate a dangerous Amanita egg)
Skin smooth, not warty
Grows from ground, not from wood
confusionWarnings: Very young Amanita eggs (buttons) can look like small puffballs — always cross-section to check for a figure inside
Smaller puffballs in the same area may be a different species
encounterRarity: Can appear in the same spot in parks or fields year after year

images.attribution: (c) Krystelle Denis, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Krystelle Denis

sourceNotes: Reliable summer and fall find in southern Ontario parks, meadows, and trail edges.

descriptionShort: Thin, fan-shaped brackets in overlapping rows on logs and stumps, with concentric bands of brown, tan, grey, and cream. One of the most common wood fungi in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Turkey Tail covers fallen logs and stumps in overlapping, fan-shaped shelves barely a few millimetres thick. The top has distinct concentric zones of colour — browns, greys, tans, creams, sometimes blue-green near the base. The edge is wavy and often paler. The underside is white or cream with fine pores, not gills. It's flexible, not brittle. One of the most reliably common mushrooms in Ontario forests year-round.
keyTraits: Concentric colour bands on top — no two are the same pattern
White or cream pored underside — fine pores, not gills
Thin and flexible, not thick and rigid
Always on wood — logs, stumps, fallen branches
expertClues: Pores are very fine — around 3–5 per millimetre
Top surface is velvety/hairy, not smooth
Several lookalikes exist — check the pore surface carefully
confusionWarnings: Stereum species look similar but have a smooth, non-pored underside
False Turkey Tail (Stereum ostrea) has a smooth orange-brown underside — no pores
encounterRarity: Found year-round; one of the most abundant woodland fungi in the region

images.attribution: (c) Christine Young, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Christine Young

sourceNotes: Ubiquitous across southern Ontario. Year-round presence makes it a reliable beginner find.

descriptionShort: Golden-yellow, vase-shaped mushrooms with wavy caps and forking ridges (not gills) on the underside. Found in summer and fall in mixed forest.
descriptionLong: Chanterelles are golden to egg-yolk yellow, with a cap that starts rounded and becomes wavy and irregular as it matures. The most important feature: the underside has forked ridges that run partway down the stem — they are blunt, not sharp, and are part of the mushroom flesh, not separate blade-like gills. The stem is solid and the same colour as the cap. They grow from soil in mixed or deciduous forest, often near oaks and beeches.
keyTraits: Golden yellow colour throughout — cap, ridges, and stem
Forking blunt ridges on underside — not true gills
Cap edge is wavy and irregular when mature
Fruity, apricot-like smell
expertClues: Ridges fork repeatedly and run down the stem (decurrent)
Flesh is white or pale yellow when cut — doesn't change colour
No ring on stem
Grows from soil, never from wood
confusionWarnings: Jack-o-lantern mushroom (Omphalotus) looks similar but has true sharp gills, grows in clusters on wood or buried roots, and glows faintly in total darkness
False chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca) has thin, crowded true gills
encounterRarity: Reliable summer and fall find in good mixed forest habitat; appears in the same spots year after year

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Classic summer-fall find in southern Ontario mixed forest. One of the most prized and distinctive finds for a young naturalist.

descriptionShort: Fan-shaped, white to grey-brown caps in overlapping clusters on dead or dying trees. Gills run down a short off-centre stem or no stem at all.
descriptionLong: Oyster Mushrooms grow in overlapping clusters on dead or dying hardwood trees. The cap is fan-shaped or oyster-shaped, smooth, white to grey or pale brown, with the edge often rolled under when young. The gills are white, close together, and run down toward the attachment point (the stem, if there is one, is short and off-centre). They can grow high up on trees or at the base of stumps. The late-season variety can appear even in cold weather.
keyTraits: Fan-shaped white to grey-brown cap
White gills that run toward the stem
Grows in clusters on dead wood
Little or no stem — attached directly or with a short side stem
expertClues: Cap surface is smooth, not scaly
Gills are white and close; they run down the stem (decurrent)
Grows strictly on wood — never from soil
Spore print is white to lilac-grey
confusionWarnings: Angel wings (Pleurocybella porrigens) are thinner, smaller, pure white — on conifer wood
Mock oyster (Phyllotopsis nidulans) is bright orange-yellow with a strong foul smell
encounterRarity: Regular fall and spring find on dead hardwood; sometimes fruits after cool rains in late summer

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Common and reliably found on dead Ontario hardwoods — especially beech and elm.

descriptionShort: A large, hard, flat shelf bracket with a brown-grey top and white underside that bruises brown when scratched. Found on hardwood stumps and trunks.
descriptionLong: Artist's Conk is one of the largest and most durable bracket fungi in Ontario. The top is hard, grey-brown, often layered and dusty with brown spores. The underside is brilliant white with very fine pores — and it permanently bruises dark brown when marked, which is why artists use it as a natural canvas. It's perennial, adding new growth each year, and can grow for decades. Old specimens can be enormous. Found on dead or dying hardwoods, especially maples and beeches.
keyTraits: Hard, woody shelf — does not flex
White pored underside that bruises brown when scratched
Grey-brown layered top, often dusty with spores
Perennial — grows bigger each year
expertClues: Brown bruising on white underside is the signature feature
Brown spore dust often visible around the base on old specimens
Grows on hardwoods — not conifers
Flesh is hard, corky, not soft
confusionWarnings: Ganoderma tsugae (Hemlock Varnish Shelf) is similar but has a shiny lacquered top and grows on conifers
encounterRarity: Perennial and stable — will be in the same place year after year

images.attribution: (c) eventupa, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by eventupa

sourceNotes: One of the most permanent fixtures in Ontario forests. Easy anchor find for young naturalists.

descriptionShort: Small white puffballs covered in tiny pyramid-shaped spines that rub off, leaving a net-like pattern. Found in clusters on forest floors and wood chips.
descriptionLong: Gem-Studded Puffballs are small — usually between golf-ball and tennis-ball sized — white to cream, with a distinctive surface covered in cone-shaped spines or warts that rub off, leaving a pitted or net-like pattern behind. They have a short stem-like base. Interior is white and firm when young, then turns olive-brown as spores develop. Found in troops on forest soil, wood chips, and decaying wood.
keyTraits: Small white puffball with spiny or warty surface
Spines rub off and leave a net-like scar pattern
Grows in groups — rarely alone
Short stem-like base
expertClues: Interior must be pure white (not yellow or showing internal structure) in young edible stage
Needle or stick poked in will show white interior vs. yellowing spore mass
Often found in big troops — look for many together
confusionWarnings: Like all puffballs: must cross-section to confirm pure white interior — rules out dangerous Amanita buttons
Earth-balls (Scleroderma) are similar but have a thick purplish-black interior and are toxic
encounterRarity: Very common in summer and fall; troops appear in mulch beds and forest edges

images.attribution: (c) Stephan Pflume, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Stephan Pflume

sourceNotes: Very common Ontario find. Good entry-level puffball for young naturalists.

descriptionShort: Dark grey to black, trumpet-shaped, hollow mushrooms that grow in clusters on forest floors. Easy to miss despite their unusual shape.
descriptionLong: Black Trumpets are hollow, funnel or trumpet-shaped mushrooms, dark grey to black, with a wavy, ragged cap edge. The outer surface (which is where spores form) is grey-black and slightly wrinkled or ridged — no true gills or pores. The inside of the trumpet is hollow all the way to the ground. Despite their striking shape and name, they are surprisingly difficult to spot because their dark colour blends perfectly with leaf litter in dim forest light. They grow in clusters on soil in deciduous and mixed forest.
keyTraits: Dark grey to black trumpet or funnel shape
Hollow all the way through
Wavy, ragged cap edge
No gills — outer surface is smooth to slightly wrinkled
expertClues: Outer surface is the spore-producing surface — look for slight ridges
Grows in clusters on soil, never on wood
Very camouflaged in leaf litter — look carefully in hardwood forest
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes in Ontario
Easy to miss because they blend into leaf litter — train your eye
encounterRarity: Locally abundant where found, but habitat-specific and easy to overlook

images.attribution: (c) Paul George, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by Paul George

sourceNotes: A special find worth real points. Teaches attention and careful looking.

descriptionShort: The iconic red-and-white-spotted toadstool of fairy tales. Toxic — and one of the most important mushrooms to recognize in Ontario.
descriptionLong: The Fly Agaric is one of the most recognizable fungi in the world: a bright red to orange-red cap covered in white warts (the remains of its egg-like covering). Young specimens emerge as a white egg from the soil, then the red cap pushes through, still covered in white patches. The gills are white and free from the stem. The stem is white with a skirt-like ring and a bulbous base with ragged white scales. Found under birch, aspen, and spruce in Ontario — a mycorrhizal partner. Toxic: causes hallucinations and illness. Not a death-cap risk but not to be eaten.
keyTraits: Bright red to orange-red cap with white wart patches
White gills — free from the stem
Skirt-like ring on white stem
Bulbous base with ragged white scales (volva)
expertClues: Warts wash off in heavy rain — may appear as plain red cap
Cap colour fades to orange or even yellow with age or sun
Volva at base is raggy, not a cup (distinguishes from Death Cap)
confusionWarnings: Toxic — do not eat
Faded or rain-washed caps can be confused with edible orange species
Young egg stage could be confused with edible puffballs — always cross-section to check
encounterRarity: Reliably found in birch and mixed forest in late summer and fall

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Common across Southern Ontario in birch and mixed forest. Critical species for safety education. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: Pure white from cap to base — beautiful and deadly. Responsible for most fatal mushroom poisonings in North America.
descriptionLong: The Destroying Angel is pure white in every part: cap, gills, ring, stem, and the distinctive cup-shaped volva at the base. The cap is smooth and white, broadly convex to flat when mature. The gills are crowded and free from the stem. The stem has a delicate skirt-like ring near the top and a prominent white cup (volva) buried at the base in the soil. It grows on soil in mixed and deciduous forest. The toxin — amatoxin — destroys the liver and kidneys, with symptoms delayed 6–24 hours after eating. There is no antidote. A single cap can kill an adult. The most important mushroom to know in Ontario.
keyTraits: Pure white throughout — cap, gills, stem, ring, and base
Cup-shaped volva (sack) at base — partially buried in soil
Skirt-like ring on stem
Deadly — do not touch and eat without washing hands
expertClues: Dig carefully to expose the cup base — it may be buried
Gills are free from the stem and crowded
White spore print
No odour when fresh — does not smell bad
confusionWarnings: DEADLY TOXIC — one of the most poisonous organisms on Earth
Can be confused with edible white mushrooms like button mushrooms — always check for cup base and free gills
Young egg stage looks like a white puffball — always cross-section before eating any puffball
No white gilled mushroom from the ground should be eaten without expert verification
encounterRarity: Present across southern Ontario in good forest habitat; not rare

images.attribution: (c) Kathie Hodge, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA)

sourceNotes: Ontario's most dangerous mushroom. Present throughout the province in summer and fall. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); North American Mycological Association poison case records.

descriptionShort: Introduced from Europe, now increasingly found in Ontario. Responsible for 90% of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. Olive-green to yellowish cap.
descriptionLong: The Death Cap was introduced to North America from Europe, likely via imported ornamental trees. It is now established and spreading in southern Ontario, particularly in association with European oaks and other introduced hardwoods in urban and suburban settings. The cap is pale olive-green to yellowish-brown, sometimes nearly white, smooth and slightly sticky when wet. Gills are white and free from the stem. The stem is white with a drooping skirt-like ring and a prominent cup-shaped volva at the base. The toxin — identical to the Destroying Angel — destroys the liver with delayed symptoms and no antidote. Finding one in Ontario is a rare and important observation.
keyTraits: Olive-green to yellowish cap — smooth and slightly sticky
White gills and white stem
Prominent cup-shaped volva at base
Drooping ring on upper stem
expertClues: More olive-coloured than the Destroying Angel — but colour varies significantly
Associated with introduced European tree species in urban parks
Cup (volva) is larger and more prominent than in most Ontario Amanitas
Report any confirmed sightings to iNaturalist Ontario to help track its spread
confusionWarnings: DEADLY TOXIC — 90% of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide
Pale forms can be confused with edible Agaricus species — always check for cup base
Has been confused with edible Paddy Straw Mushroom (Volvariella) by Asian foragers unfamiliar with it
encounterRarity: Increasingly found in southern Ontario urban parks near European oaks; formerly very rare

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Introduced species now established in southern Ontario. Sightings increasing near Toronto and Hamilton. Reference: iNaturalist Ontario records; MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A common Amanita with a pinkish-brown cap and grey-pink warts. The flesh turns pink when cut or bruised — hence the name. Edible only when thoroughly cooked.
descriptionLong: The Blusher is one of the most common Amanitas in Ontario forests. The cap is pinkish-brown to reddish-brown, covered in dirty grey or pinkish warts that may wash off in rain. The critical identifying feature is the flesh: when cut, it slowly turns pink or reddish — this 'blushing' is unique among Ontario Amanitas. Gills are white, becoming slightly pinkish. The stem has a ring and a bulbous base with a raggy, not cup-shaped, volva. It grows in both conifer and deciduous forest. It is toxic when raw (contains haemolysin), but the toxin is destroyed by thorough cooking. However, given its Amanita identity, it should only be considered by experts.
keyTraits: Pinkish-brown cap with grey or pinkish warts
Flesh turns pink when cut or bruised — the 'blush'
Raggy (not cup-shaped) base on stem
Ring on stem, gills white to pinkish
expertClues: Cut the stem — reddening of the flesh is diagnostic
Warts are more grey or dirty-pink than pure white (unlike Fly Agaric)
Very common — can be abundant in good years
confusionWarnings: Toxic raw — haemolysin destroyed only by thorough cooking; beginners should not harvest
Confused with Panther Cap (Amanita pantherina) — which does NOT blush and is seriously toxic
Confused with other brown Amanitas — always check for the blushing reaction
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario forests — one of the most frequently encountered Amanitas

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Very common Southern Ontario Amanita. Blushing reaction is key to identification. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Audubon Society Field Guide.

descriptionShort: A seriously toxic brown Amanita with white warts and a distinctive ridged ring around the stem. Often confused with the edible Blusher.
descriptionLong: The Panther Cap is one of the more dangerous Amanitas in Ontario — toxic enough to cause serious illness. The cap is brown to grey-brown with pure white warts, which are more regular and evenly spaced than on the Blusher. The key differences from the Blusher: the flesh does NOT blush when cut — it stays white. The ring on the stem is typically striate (has ridges on the upper surface). The base has a bulbous volva with one or two distinct ridges around it, like a collar. Found in both deciduous and mixed forest. Toxins include ibotenic acid and muscimol — cause nausea, hallucinations, and serious illness.
keyTraits: Brown to grey-brown cap with regular white warts
Flesh stays WHITE when cut — does not blush
Ring on stem has ridges (striate) on upper surface
Bulbous base with collar-like ridges around it
expertClues: Cut the stem base to check for blushing — no colour change confirms Panther Cap over Blusher
Warts are pure white and more uniform than Blusher's grey-pink warts
Ring is positioned higher on stem than Blusher's ring
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — ibotenic acid and muscimol cause hallucinations and serious illness
Critically confused with the Blusher (Amanita rubescens) — always cut and check for blushing
Also confused with other brown-capped Amanitas
encounterRarity: Less common than Blusher but present in Ontario mixed forest; encountered occasionally

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Present but less common than Blusher in southern Ontario. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society records.

descriptionShort: A vivid orange mushroom that grows in large clusters at the base of trees. Its gills glow faintly in total darkness — and it's toxic.
descriptionLong: Jack-o'-Lanterns are large, vivid orange mushrooms that grow in tight clusters at the base of trees or from buried roots — they appear to grow from soil but are actually connected to wood underground. The cap is orange, smooth, convex to vase-shaped. The gills are orange, crowded, and run slightly down the stem. The most famous feature: the gills contain a compound (luciferase) that glows faintly green in total darkness — you need complete darkness and eyes adapted for several minutes to see it. Toxic: causes severe gastrointestinal illness. Commonly mistaken for chanterelles.
keyTraits: Vivid orange throughout — cap, gills, and stem
Grows in dense clusters at tree base or from buried roots
True sharp gills (not forked ridges like chanterelle)
Gills glow faintly green in complete darkness
expertClues: Always clustered — chanterelles grow singly or in small loose groups
Connected to wood underground — dig gently to confirm
Gills are sharp and crowded; chanterelle ridges are blunt and forked
Strong unpleasant smell in large clusters
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — causes severe vomiting and diarrhoea
Most commonly confused with Chanterelles — check for sharp gills vs. blunt forked ridges
Chanterelles grow singly from soil; Jack-o'-Lanterns cluster from wood
encounterRarity: Found reliably at the base of oaks and maples in Ontario forests from summer through fall

images.attribution: (c) Jason Hollinger, some rights reserved (CC BY)

sourceNotes: Common at oak bases across southern Ontario. Important safety species — the number one chanterelle lookalike. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A tall, white, shaggy-scaled mushroom that digests itself into black ink within hours of picking. Grows in lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground.
descriptionLong: Shaggy Manes are tall, cylindrical mushrooms with a white cap covered in shaggy brown-tipped scales — giving them the look of a dishevelled barrister's wig. The gills start white and crowded, then begin to auto-digest from the edge inward, turning pink, then black and dripping, until the entire cap dissolves into inky fluid within hours. This process (autodigestion) is how spores are dispersed. They grow in lawns, roadsides, gravel paths, parks, and disturbed ground — often in lines or troops. They should be harvested and eaten immediately if at all — they will self-digest in your bag.
keyTraits: Tall white cylinder with shaggy brown-tipped scales
Gills auto-digest to black dripping ink from cap edge inward
Grows in disturbed ground, lawns, and roadsides
No volva at base — grows straight from the ground
expertClues: Autodigestion happens fast — pick and use within the hour for edibility
Often grows in lines following buried organic material
The dissolving cap edge is diagnostic — no other large white mushroom does this
confusionWarnings: Do not eat with alcohol — may cause flushing reaction similar to Inky Cap (Coprinellus micaceus)
Confirming species before eating is straightforward due to the unique auto-digestion
encounterRarity: Appears reliably in lawns and disturbed ground across Ontario from spring through fall

images.attribution: (c) Tom Norton, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Tom Norton

sourceNotes: Very common Ontario find in disturbed habitats. Autodigestion makes it unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: One of the most common mushrooms in Ontario — grows in large clusters at the base of trees and stumps. Honey-coloured caps, white ring on stem.
descriptionLong: Honey Mushrooms are abundant throughout Ontario, fruiting in spectacular clusters at the base of living trees, stumps, and from buried roots. The cap is honey-yellow to tawny or tawny-brown, often with darker scales or fibres toward the centre. The gills are white becoming pale tan or spotted. The stem has a distinctive white ring (often with a yellowish edge) and is fibrous. Honey Mushrooms are parasitic on living trees and can spread through forest soil via black root-like cords (rhizomorphs). Some specimens of Armillaria ostoyae in North America are among the largest living organisms on Earth by weight. The species complex is edible when cooked — but causes illness when raw or undercooked.
keyTraits: Honey-yellow to tawny cap, often with dark fibrous scales at centre
White ring on stem
Grows in large clusters at tree bases and stumps
White to pale tan gills
expertClues: Black rhizomorphs (root-like cords) may be visible at the base
Highly variable appearance — 'Honey Mushroom' covers a complex of species
Ring may degrade and appear raggedy on older specimens
Gills sometimes develop brown spots with age
confusionWarnings: Several toxic species look similar — Galerina marginata grows in similar habitat and has a ring; it is deadly
Always confirm: Honey Mushroom has white spore print; Galerina has rusty-brown spore print
Do not eat raw — causes illness; thorough cooking required
encounterRarity: Extremely common across Ontario in fall — one of the most abundant autumn fungi

images.attribution: (c) Nathan Wilson, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

sourceNotes: Among the most common fall fungi in Ontario. Species complex — Armillaria mellea, gallica, solidipes all present. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A small orange-yellow mushroom that fruits in winter on dead elms and other hardwoods. The wild form of the cultivated enoki mushroom.
descriptionLong: Velvet Shank is one of the few mushrooms that fruits in late fall and winter in Ontario — often emerging through snow. The cap is smooth, glossy, orange to orange-yellow, darker in the centre and paler at the edges. Gills are cream to pale yellow, fairly well-spaced. The stem is the give-away: it is dark brown to black at the base, becoming orange upward, and has a distinctly velvety texture from fine dark hairs. It grows in clusters on dead or dying elms, ashes, and other hardwoods. The cultivated form (enoki) looks completely different — long white strands — because it's grown in the dark with high CO2.
keyTraits: Glossy orange cap, paler at edges
Stem dark brown to black at base, velvety from fine hairs
Fruits in late fall and winter — even through snow
Clusters on dead elm and other hardwoods
expertClues: The velvety dark base of the stem is unmistakeable
Winter fruiting in Ontario is nearly unique among gilled mushrooms
Pale cream to yellow gills, not white
confusionWarnings: Could be confused with Galerina marginata (deadly) when old or weathered — check for velvety stem base
Honey Mushroom is similar but lacks the dark velvety stem base and fruits in fall, not winter
encounterRarity: Reliable on dead elms throughout Ontario from October through February

images.attribution: (c) KimberlySteinmann, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by KimberlySteinmann

sourceNotes: Reliable winter find on dead Ontario elms — the wild ancestor of cultivated enoki. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: Small tawny-brown mushrooms with mica-like glistening particles on the cap. Grows in clusters on stumps and buried wood, and partially self-digests with age.
descriptionLong: The Inky Cap is one of the most common small mushrooms on wood in Ontario. Caps are oval when young, expanding to bell-shaped, tawny-brown with a glistening surface from tiny mica-like particles (which may wash off). The edges become pleated (striate) as the cap opens. The gills start white, then grey, then auto-digest to black but less dramatically than the Shaggy Mane. Grows in dense clusters on or near stumps, fallen logs, and buried wood — often in parks and urban areas. Contains coprine, which inhibits alcohol metabolism — eating it within 48–72 hours of drinking alcohol causes flushing, nausea, and rapid heartbeat.
keyTraits: Tawny-brown cap with mica-like glistening surface
Pleated (striate) cap edge when open
Dense clusters on stumps and buried wood
Gills turn grey then black with age
expertClues: Mica-like particles may wash off in rain — compare with fresh specimens
Often found in spectacular clusters of dozens of caps
Coprine reaction with alcohol — important even for foragers
confusionWarnings: Do not consume with alcohol — coprine causes alcohol flush reaction up to 3 days after eating
Less dramatically auto-digesting than Shaggy Mane but same caution applies — use quickly
encounterRarity: Extremely common on stumps and buried wood throughout Ontario from spring to fall

images.attribution: (c) Autumn, all rights reserved, uploaded by Autumn

sourceNotes: One of the most common small mushrooms on dead wood in Ontario. Important to know about coprine/alcohol interaction. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A large, wine-red to burgundy mushroom that appears in wood chip mulch beds, gardens, and forest edges. One of the most striking park finds in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Wine Caps are large and robust, with a smooth wine-red to burgundy-brown cap that fades to tan as it ages. The gills start grey-white, then turn dark purplish-grey as the purple-black spores mature. The stem is white, firm, and has a distinctive double-edged ring. Wine Caps are cultivated by gardeners and occasionally naturalize into mulch beds and wood chip paths in parks. They grow from soil rich in woody debris — garden beds, trail mulch, and forest edges. A very photogenic and rewarding find for any young naturalist.
keyTraits: Wine-red to burgundy cap — fading to tan with age
Gills turn dark purplish-grey as spores mature
Distinctive double-edged ring on white stem
Found in wood chip mulch and rich organic soil
expertClues: Spore print is dark purple-black
Cap can become quite large — 15–30 cm across in good conditions
Ring is white with a cogwheel-like gear edge on the underside
confusionWarnings: Generally distinctive when fresh; old faded specimens could be confused with other brown-capped species
Always confirm the gear-edged ring and dark spore print
encounterRarity: Turns up reliably wherever fresh wood chips or mulch have been spread — common in provincial park campgrounds

images.attribution: (c) tchitcherine, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

sourceNotes: Commonly found in wood chip mulch across southern Ontario parks and gardens. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: The wild ancestor of the grocery-store button mushroom. Found in fields and lawns. Pink gills when young — a key safety identifier.
descriptionLong: The Meadow Mushroom is a medium-sized grassland mushroom with a white to grey-brown cap, firm white flesh, and pink to chocolate-brown gills that darken as the black-brown spores mature. The smell is pleasantly mushroomy. It has a simple ring on the stem and no volva at the base. It grows in grassy areas, fields, and lawns — often in fairy rings or arcs. It is closely related to the cultivated button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus). Identification key: the gills must be pink in young specimens — never white. White gills in a button-shaped white mushroom in grass could indicate a deadly Amanita.
keyTraits: White to grey-brown cap
Gills pink in young specimens — darkening to chocolate-brown

sourceNotes: Classic Ontario field mushroom. Safety note on pink gills vs white is critical for young observers. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Audubon Society Field Guide.

descriptionShort: One of the most startling mushrooms in Ontario — it bleeds vivid blue milk when cut. The cap and gills are blue-grey. Unmistakeable.
descriptionLong: The Indigo Milk Cap is one of the most visually striking fungi in eastern North America. The cap is blue-grey with faint concentric zones, often with a green tinge on older specimens. The gills are indigo blue. When any part is cut, broken, or bruised, it immediately exudes dark indigo-blue latex (milk) that slowly turns green as it oxidizes. The flesh itself is blue when cut. It grows from soil in mixed forest, particularly under oaks and pines, forming mycorrhizal relationships. Edible and considered a choice find — the blue milk is completely unmistakeable and makes safe identification straightforward.

Bleeds vivid indigo-blue latex (milk) when cut — turns green as it dries
Blue gills
Grows from soil under oaks and pines

Older caps develop a greenish patina
Firm, dense flesh; no ring or volva
Spore print pale yellow to cream
confusionWarnings: Essentially no dangerous lookalikes given the blue milk — visually unmistakeable when fresh
Old or dried specimens may lose the blue colour — check for faint blue-green staining
encounterRarity: Present in suitable Ontario habitat but not abundant — a memorable find

sourceNotes: Southern Ontario distribution centred in the mixed forest zone. Present but local. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society records.

descriptionShort: Not a mushroom at all — it's a parasitic mould that covers a host mushroom in vivid orange-red, transforming it completely. Looks like a lobster claw.
descriptionLong: The Lobster Mushroom is technically a parasitic ascomycete fungus (Hypomyces lactifluorum) that attacks Lactarius or Russula species, completely covering the host mushroom in a hard, orange-red skin. The surface becomes dense and textured with tiny pores where the parasite produces spores. The original host's cap, gills, and stem are completely encased and distorted — the resulting shape is lumpy and irregular, reminiscent of a cooked lobster. The flesh inside is white and firm. It is commonly collected as an edible (the parasite plus host) and has a pleasant, mild flavour. Found in mixed forest where Lactarius and Russula grow.
keyTraits: Vivid orange-red hard outer skin covering entire mushroom
Lumpy, distorted shape — original cap and gills hidden
White firm flesh inside
Found on soil in forest — parasite of Lactarius or Russula

Host species identity is obscured — cannot determine what is underneath
Firm and dense throughout — not hollow
confusionWarnings: Ensure the entire surface is uniformly orange-red — partial infection or other orange fungi could cause confusion
Some experts avoid collecting as host identity cannot be confirmed; risk is very low but non-zero
encounterRarity: Found in suitable mixed forest habitat across Ontario; not uncommon in good years

images.attribution: (c) tombigelow, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by tombigelow

sourceNotes: Distinctive and memorable Ontario find. Parasite of Lactarius/Russula in mixed forest. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A striking purple-lilac mushroom that appears in late fall in leaf litter and garden compost. One of the last mushrooms of the season.
descriptionLong: Blewits are distinctive for their overall purple-lilac to violet colouration — cap, gills, and stem are all tinged lilac, though this fades with age to buff or brownish. The cap is smooth, convex at first then broad and wavy. Gills are crowded and slightly notched at the stem, lilac to pale purple. The stem is solid, fibrous, and often with a bulbous base. They grow in late fall in leaf litter, compost heaps, and forest edges — often appearing after the first frosts when most other mushrooms have finished. Edible and popular with foragers, but must be cooked. The lilac colouration in late fall leaf litter is unique.

Late fall fruiting — often after first frost

sourceNotes: Late-season find across southern Ontario. Colour is distinctive but Cortinarius confusion requires caution. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A brown, velvety-stemmed mushroom with a rolled cap edge and crowded forked gills. Grows on conifer stumps and logs.
descriptionLong: The Velvet Roll-rim has a distinctive chocolate to coffee-brown cap, convex and wavy, with the cap edge distinctly rolled under when young. The underside has crowded, forking, cream to pale yellow gills that run slightly down the stem. The most distinctive feature is the stem: covered in dense dark brown to black velvety hairs (tomentum), unlike almost any other species. It grows on or at the base of conifer stumps and logs — especially pine. A large, robust mushroom that makes a striking find. Not edible.

sourceNotes: Common Ontario species on pine and spruce stumps. Velvety stem is diagnostic. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A vivid orange-yellow fan-shaped mushroom on dead wood with a famously strong, unpleasant smell. Impossible to miss, impossible to forget.
descriptionLong: The Orange Mock Oyster is visually stunning: fan-shaped, bright egg-yolk orange to orange-yellow, with a surface covered in fine hairs. The gills are also orange. It has little or no stem and grows directly from dead wood in overlapping clusters. The most memorable feature is the smell: it is extremely strong and unpleasant — often described as rotten cabbage or skunk. Despite the colour resemblance to Chanterelles and edible oyster mushrooms, the smell alone makes it unforgettable. Not edible. Found year-round on dead hardwood.

Orange gills

Gills are also orange, matching the cap

Colour could suggest edible species but smell immediately separates it
encounterRarity: Regular on dead wood across Ontario in fall; can persist through mild winters

sourceNotes: Common on Ontario dead hardwood. The smell is the definitive field identifier. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A tiny reddish-brown bell-capped mushroom that bleeds dark red-burgundy latex from the stem when broken. Grows in clusters on rotting logs.
descriptionLong: The Bleeding Mycena is a small, fragile mushroom with a bell-shaped cap in shades of reddish-brown or pinkish-brown, with a striate (grooved) edge that shows the gill positions beneath. Gills are white to pinkish and attached to the stem. The stem is slender, smooth, and the same colour as the cap. When the stem is broken, it exudes a distinctive dark red to burgundy latex — a remarkable feature for such a tiny mushroom. They grow in small clusters from the sides of rotting hardwood logs and stumps. Common but easy to overlook unless you're looking closely at dead wood.

encounterRarity: Very common on rotting hardwood logs across Ontario; requires looking closely at log surfaces

sourceNotes: Very common on Ontario rotting hardwood. Red bleeding makes identification straightforward. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A sharply conical red-orange waxcap that slowly turns completely black as it ages. Found in undisturbed grasslands — an indicator of ancient meadow habitat.
descriptionLong: Witch's Hat is one of the most visually dramatic small mushrooms in Ontario. The cap is sharply conical — like a pointed hat — vivid orange-red to scarlet, with a distinctly waxy texture. As the mushroom matures, it begins to turn black, starting at the cap tip and spreading downward until the entire mushroom is black and slimy. Gills are yellow-orange. The stem is smooth, hollow, and the same colour as the cap. Waxcaps are indicators of ecological quality: finding Witch's Hat in a grassland suggests the soil has remained undisturbed for decades. It is not common in managed Ontario parks but appears in old meadows and unimproved grasslands.

Waxy texture on cap surface
Indicator of ancient, undisturbed grassland

encounterRarity: Uncommon; restricted to high-quality undisturbed grassland in Ontario — a meaningful ecological find

sourceNotes: Ecological indicator of old-growth grassland in Ontario. Present but not common. Reference: Ontario Mycological Society; MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A small salmon-pink to peach-coloured mushroom with a uniquely wrinkled and netted cap surface. Found only on dead elm. Globally declining.
descriptionLong: The Wrinkled Peach is one of the most beautiful fungi in Ontario — and one of the rarest. The cap is salmon-pink to peach-coral, with a surface covered in a raised network of ridges and veins giving it a honeycomb or brain-like texture. The gills are salmon-pink and connected across adjacent gills by veins. The stem is off-centre, thick, and the same colour as the cap. It grows exclusively on large dead elm logs and stumps. It is declining across North America and Europe due to the loss of American Elm from Dutch Elm Disease — the same disease that devastated Ontario's elm forests. Finding one is a genuine red-letter day.

Veins connecting the gills
Grows only on dead elm logs

encounterRarity: Rare; associated with declining elm habitat in Ontario. A very special find worth reporting to iNaturalist

sourceNotes: Declining globally due to elm loss. Present in Ontario wherever large elm logs remain. Report sightings to iNaturalist Ontario. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: The most prized wild mushroom in the world. A large, stout bolete with a chestnut-brown cap, cream to yellow-green pores, and a pale, net-patterned stem.
descriptionLong: The King Bolete is the most celebrated edible wild mushroom in the world — known as porcini in Italy, cep in France, and Steinpilz in Germany. The cap is chestnut-brown to tan, smooth and slightly sticky when wet, convex becoming broadly flat. The underside has fine cream to yellow-green pores (not gills) that do not stain blue when cut. The stem is pale tan to cream, stout and club-shaped, covered in a fine white network pattern (reticulation). Flesh is white and firm, does not change colour when cut. Found from soil in mixed forest under beech, birch, spruce, and hemlock. One of Ontario's most sought-after finds.

Stout cream stem with fine white net pattern (reticulation)
Flesh white and firm — does not blue when cut
No ring; grows from soil in mixed forest

encounterRarity: Present in Ontario under birch and hemlock; good years produce many finds; requires learning the habitat

sourceNotes: Present across southern Ontario under birch, beech, and hemlock. Peak season July–September. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Audubon Society Field Guide.

descriptionShort: A very common bolete under conifers, with a bay-brown cap and yellow pores that stain blue-green when touched. One of Ontario's most abundant boletes.
descriptionLong: The Bay Bolete is one of the most frequently encountered boletes in Ontario, particularly common under pine, spruce, and fir. The cap is bay-brown to dark reddish-brown, smooth to slightly tacky. The pores are cream to yellow, and they stain blue-green when pressed or cut — a helpful identifier. The stem is pale brown, streaked, cylindrical, and lacks the reticulate network of the King Bolete. Flesh is pale, staining blue-green when cut. Found in conifer and mixed forest from summer through fall. Considered edible and pleasant, though the blue-staining causes hesitation in beginners.

Yellow pores that stain blue-green when pressed

sourceNotes: Very common Ontario bolete under conifers. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A striking bolete with a dark red cap and bright yellow pores — contrasting two-tone colouring makes it one of the easiest boletes to identify.
descriptionLong: The Two-colored Bolete is one of the more visually dramatic boletes in Ontario: the cap is dark blood-red to rose-red, while the underside has vivid yellow pores — creating a bold two-tone contrast. The stem is red above, yellow at the base, and often streaked. When cut, the pores and stem flesh slowly blush blue, though less intensely than some other species. The flesh itself is pale yellow. It grows from soil under oaks and mixed hardwoods in summer and early fall. A rewarding and relatively easy bolete to identify confidently.

Bright yellow pores — contrasting two-tone

Slow, mild blue staining when cut

sourceNotes: Summer bolete under Ontario oaks. Two-tone colour is distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A streaky red-brown Suillus with a fibrous cap and large pores. Found exclusively under Eastern White Pine — follow the pines to find it.
descriptionLong: The Painted Suillus is a medium-sized bolete found only under Eastern White Pine — it is a strict mycorrhizal partner of this tree. The cap is reddish-brown, covered in darker red-brown fibrous scales or streaks that peel back from the margin, giving it a shaggy, painted appearance. The pores are large and yellow, often reddening slightly. The stem is yellow above the ring, reddish below. A partial veil covers young pores and leaves a ring on the stem. Frequent in Ontario pine forests and plantations. Not considered prime edible material — the Suillus family is generally poor in flavour and texture.

Only under Eastern White Pine — substrate is diagnostic

sourceNotes: Strict associate of Eastern White Pine. Very common in Ontario pine habitat. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A slimy brown-capped bolete under introduced pines, named for its famously sticky cap. One of the most frequently encountered boletes in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Slippery Jack has one of the most distinctive caps in the bolete world: it is covered in a thick, slippery-slimy layer of brown to chocolate-brown slime that coats the entire cap surface and makes it slick to the touch, especially in wet weather. The pores underneath are yellow and fine, and there is a large, baggy, dark purple-grey ring on the stem. Below the ring the stem is darker and dotted with small glandular spots. Found under pine — particularly Scots Pine and other two-needle pines. Technically edible but the slimy cap should be peeled before cooking.

Yellow pores

descriptionShort: A shaggy black-and-white bolete covered in thick woolly scales — looks like it belongs in a fairy tale. Unmistakeable.
descriptionLong: Old Man of the Woods is one of the most distinctive boletes in Ontario — shaggy, dramatic, and unmistakeable. The cap is covered in thick, woolly black or dark grey scales against a white background, giving it a striking salt-and-pepper or pine cone appearance. The pores are white to grey, turning dark when touched. The stem is shaggy with similar dark scales and may have a partial veil remnant near the top. The flesh is white, quickly staining red then black when cut. Found in mixed hardwood and hardwood-hemlock forest, growing from soil. Not a culinary prize but a showstopper find.

descriptionShort: A stunning bolete with a vivid scarlet-red reticulate stem and a yellow-brown cap. One of the most visually striking boletes in Ontario.
descriptionLong: The Scarlet-stemmed Bolete is among the most beautiful boletes in Ontario. The cap is yellow-brown to ochre, smooth, convex. The pores are yellow and may slowly blush blue. But the stem is the spectacle: brilliant scarlet-red, covered in a raised network pattern (reticulation) that makes it look carved or textured. The stem is slender compared to the cap. Found under beech and birch in mixed forest. Not common but unforgettable when encountered — a standout even among experienced foragers.

descriptionShort: A yellow-capped bolete with bright yellow pores and yellow flesh that turns blue when cut. Found under beech and oak.
descriptionLong: The Butter Bolete is a handsome, medium to large bolete with a yellow-brown to buff cap and vivid bright yellow pores. The stem is yellow and covered in a fine yellow network. The flesh is bright yellow throughout and turns blue relatively quickly when cut — a dramatic contrast. Found under beech, oak, and other hardwoods in summer and fall in southern Ontario. The yellow pores and yellow flesh with a blue staining reaction are distinctive. Not common but a rewarding find.

descriptionShort: A massive, overlapping rosette of grey-brown fronds at the base of old oaks. One of Ontario's greatest culinary finds — known as maitake in Japan.
descriptionLong: Hen of the Woods is one of the most impressive fungi in Ontario — a sprawling, overlapping mass of fan-shaped fronds arising from a shared white base, forming a rosette that can weigh several kilograms. Individual fronds are grey-brown, smooth, with wavy edges. The undersides are white with fine pores. It grows at the base of living or recently dead oaks — often the same tree every year. In Japan it is cultivated as maitake — one of the world's most prized edible and medicinal fungi. Finding a large one is a genuine landmark moment in any season.

descriptionShort: A large, saddle-shaped bracket with a distinctive scaly brown-and-cream pattern and white pores. Common in spring on dead hardwoods.
descriptionLong: Dryad's Saddle is one of spring's most reliable and recognizable fungi. The cap is large, kidney-shaped to fan-shaped, pale cream to tan with dark brown scales arranged in a concentric pattern — resembling the feathers of a pheasant. The underside has large white to cream pores. It has a short, thick, off-centre stem. The smell is distinctive and pleasant — often described as watermelon rind or cucumber. Young, tender specimens are edible; older ones become tough and fibrous. Found on dead or dying hardwoods, especially elm, maple, and box elder, from spring through early summer.

Pleasant watermelon-rind or cucumber smell

sourceNotes: One of the most common spring polypores in Ontario. Cucumber smell is distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A smooth, kidney-shaped white to grey-brown bracket found exclusively on birch trees. Present year-round — one of the easiest Ontario polypores to identify.
descriptionLong: The Birch Polypore is a smooth, hoof-shaped or kidney-shaped annual bracket found only on birch. The top surface is smooth, pale grey-brown to grey-white, with a rounded, inrolled margin. The underside is white with very fine pores. The flesh is white and firm with a slightly rubbery texture. It grows on both living and dead birches, causing brown rot. It is annual — new brackets form each year, unlike Artist's Conk. Historically used as a razor strop (to sharpen blades), as a wound dressing, and found with Ötzi the Iceman. Year-round presence on birch makes it a reliable anchor find.

Inrolled pale margin
Only on birch trees
Year-round; annual bracket

descriptionShort: A large, perennial conk with a distinctive red-orange banding near the margin. Found on conifer logs and stumps throughout Ontario.
descriptionLong: The Red-belted Polypore is one of the most common large conks in Ontario conifer forest. It is perennial — adding a new layer of growth each year — and can grow very large over time. The top is hard and woody, with concentric zones in shades of orange-red, brown, and grey. The most distinctive feature is the bright red-orange band near the growing margin, which is pale yellow to cream on very fresh specimens. The underside has cream-white fine pores. It causes brown rot in conifers. Found on spruce, fir, pine, and occasionally hardwoods. A year-round presence.

Red-orange banding near the growing margin
White to cream fine pores underneath
Concentric zones of orange, brown, grey on top
expertClues: Red margin band brightens in active growing season; dulls and darkens with age
Hard and heavy — substantial conk
On conifers — distinguishes it from Artist's Conk (hardwoods)

Ganoderma tsugae (Reishi relative) has a lacquered, shiny reddish top
encounterRarity: Abundant on dead conifer logs throughout Ontario — a year-round fixture of the forest floor

sourceNotes: Common year-round on Ontario conifer logs. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A stunning lacquered shelf fungus on hemlock — shiny, kidney-shaped, red-orange with a cream margin. One of the most striking fungi in Ontario forests.
descriptionLong: Ganoderma tsugae is the North American counterpart to the famous medicinal Reishi (G. lingzhi) of East Asia. It grows on hemlock — both living and dead — and produces a beautiful, kidney-shaped bracket with a brilliant lacquered surface in shades of red, orange, and mahogany, fading to cream at the growing margin. The underside is white to cream with very fine pores. The stem (when present) is also lacquered red. The surface feels hard and polished like varnished wood. It is annual and often found when young and actively growing in June and July, when the cream margin is at its most vivid. Not edible fresh, but deeply embedded in traditional medicine.
keyTraits: Brilliant lacquered shiny surface — red, orange, mahogany
Cream to white growing margin when active
Only on hemlock trees
White fine pores underneath
expertClues: Lacquered surface distinguishes it from all other Ontario polypores
Strictly on hemlock — substrate is diagnostic
Annual; new bracket each year; old ones dull and fade
confusionWarnings: Artist's Conk (Ganoderma applanatum) is similar but not lacquered and grows on hardwoods
G. applanatum has brown spore dust on top; Reishi does not when fresh
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario hemlock stands; not rare in good hemlock forest but habitat-restricted

images.attribution: (c) smoore13, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

sourceNotes: Found in Ontario hemlock stands from Lake Simcoe south. Lacquered surface unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A vivid orange-red bracket fungus that looks spray-painted. One of the most photogenic fungi in the Ontario woods.
descriptionLong: The Cinnabar Polypore is instantly recognizable by its brilliant cinnabar-orange to red-orange colour — the entire bracket, top and bottom, is the same vivid shade. The top surface has faint concentric zones and is slightly rough to touch. The pores underneath are also orange, though usually slightly lighter. It grows on dead hardwood — often fallen birch, maple, or aspen. The colour fades significantly with age and weathering. It is annual. A small bracket, rarely exceeding 10 cm wide, but its colour makes it visible from metres away. Not edible — flesh is too tough.
keyTraits: Brilliant cinnabar orange-red — entire bracket the same vivid colour
Both top and pore surface are orange
Small to medium fan-shaped bracket on dead hardwood
Colour fades dramatically with age
expertClues: Fresh specimens are spectacularly bright — faded ones look much less impressive
Pore surface orange (not white or cream like most polypores)
Annual; find fresh ones in early summer
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — colour is unmistakeable on fresh specimens
Chicken of the Woods is also orange but much larger and grows differently
encounterRarity: Present across Ontario on dead hardwood; not abundant but reliably found with good searching

images.attribution: (c) melissahalton, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

sourceNotes: Present on dead Ontario hardwood. Vivid colour is unmistakeable when fresh. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A large brain-shaped puffball with a wrinkled, ridged outer surface. Found in hardwood forest and forest edges.
descriptionLong: The Skull-shaped Puffball is a large puffball with a distinctive wrinkled, lobed, or ridged surface that gives it a brain-like or skull-like appearance. It grows larger than a baseball and can approach the size of a softball. The outer skin is pale tan to brown, with a thick rind. Like other puffballs, it is edible when young and completely white inside — the white interior is a food safety requirement. As it matures, the interior turns yellow then olive and finally brown, releasing spores through a pore at the top. Found in mixed and deciduous forest and forest edges.
keyTraits: Large puffball with wrinkled, ridged, brain-like surface
Pale tan to brown rind
Interior white when young
Forest habitat — not open fields like Giant Puffball
expertClues: Cross-section required before any use — must be pure white throughout
Wrinkled rind surface distinguishes it from the smooth Giant Puffball
Grows singly from soil; usually under hardwoods
confusionWarnings: Like all puffballs: cross-section to rule out Amanita buttons
Scleroderma (earthballs) are similar size but have a thick purplish-black interior — toxic
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario forest and forest edges; not as common as Gem-Studded but reliably found

images.attribution: (c) Sindhu Ramchandran, the mushroom was identified by Mr Marco Della Maggiora, Italy, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

sourceNotes: Forest puffball found across southern Ontario. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A round, yellow-brown, thick-skinned fungus with a purple-black interior. Toxic — and a common lookalike for edible puffballs.
descriptionLong: The Pigskin Poison Puffball (earthball) looks superficially like a puffball but is very different inside: the skin is thick and tough, yellowish-brown with coarse warts or cracks, and when cut the interior is dark purplish-black or purple-grey — even in young specimens. This purple interior is diagnostic and immediately distinguishes it from any edible puffball (which must have pure white flesh). It grows on soil or in sandy, disturbed ground, often in association with roots of oaks and birch. It is toxic and should never be eaten. Teaching the cross-section test to young naturalists is the key safety lesson here.
keyTraits: Yellow-brown to tan outer skin with coarse warts or cracks
Interior is purple to purple-black — NOT white
Thick, tough outer rind
Toxic
expertClues: Cut it open — purple-black interior identifies it immediately
Rind is noticeably thicker and tougher than any edible puffball
Often found near tree roots — partial mycorrhizal association
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — causes severe gastrointestinal illness
Superficially resembles edible puffballs but purple interior is immediately diagnostic
The cross-section test is mandatory before consuming any puffball-like fungus
encounterRarity: Common in sandy soil and woodland paths across Ontario

images.attribution: (c) Jason M Crockwell, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND), uploaded by Jason M Crockwell

sourceNotes: Very common in sandy Ontario soil. The cross-section test is the primary safety teaching point. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: An upright white to cream coral fungus with each branch tip ending in a tiny crown of 3–6 small points. A reliable and distinctive Ontario species.
descriptionLong: The Crowned Coral is one of Ontario's most reliable coral fungi. It grows upright in clusters from dead hardwood, cream to pale tan or yellowish, with branches that repeatedly fork upward. The identifying feature is at the branch tips: each tip ends in a tiny cup or crown with 3 to 6 small points — like a miniature crown or corolla. This feature is visible to the naked eye with a close look. It grows from dead logs, stumps, and buried wood debris in hardwood and mixed forest. Considered edible but not of high quality.
keyTraits: Upright branching coral form, cream to pale tan
Each branch tip ends in a tiny 3–6-pointed crown
Grows from dead hardwood — not soil
Brittle and fragile
expertClues: Crown tips are visible to the naked eye — inspect with a hand lens for detail
Grows from wood, not soil — distinguishes it from soil-based Ramaria species
Usually cream to pale tan, rarely yellowish

Yellow or orange corals from soil require more careful identification — some Ramaria may be mildly toxic
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario hardwood forest on dead logs; encountered regularly with attentive searching

images.attribution: (c) Adam Bryant, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Adam Bryant

sourceNotes: Common on Ontario dead hardwood logs. Crown tips are diagnostic. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: An enormous, wavy, cream-coloured mass of flattened fronds at the base of pine trees. Can weigh several kilograms. Unmistakeable.
descriptionLong: The Cauliflower Mushroom is one of the most dramatic fungi in Ontario. It grows as a massive, densely layered mass of flat, wavy, cream to pale yellow fronds arising from a shared rooting base at the base of pine trees. The whole structure resembles a large head of cauliflower or brain coral. It can grow to 60 cm across and weigh several kilograms. The fronds are thin, wavy, and elastic. It grows at the base of living pines, arising from the roots. Edible and highly regarded when young and fresh. The rooting habit at the base of pine is distinctive.
keyTraits: Enormous wavy cream frond mass at pine tree base
Flat, wavy, leaf-like fronds densely packed
Rooting base in pine root zone
Can be very large — up to 60 cm across
expertClues: Always at the base of pine — substrate is key
Fronds are flat and wavy, not cylindrical like coral fungi
Often returns to the same tree in successive years
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes when mature — appearance is highly distinctive
Old specimens become brown and unpleasant; harvest only cream-coloured young individuals
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario pine forest but not common — a very memorable find when encountered

images.attribution: (c) Evan M. Raskin, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Evan M. Raskin

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario pine stands. Base-of-pine habitat is diagnostic. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A branching coral fungus with pale tan to brownish branches tipped in distinctive violet-purple. Grows from soil in mixed forest.
descriptionLong: Violet-tipped Coral is one of the more identifiable Ramaria species in Ontario because of its distinctive colouration: the branches are pale tan to brownish, but the branch tips are tinted violet or purple. It grows from soil in mixed forest, often under conifers or in mixed conifer-hardwood stands. Ramaria species generally require cautious identification — some are mildly toxic. The violet tips are a useful field mark but can fade in old specimens. Grows in summer and fall.

Grows from soil — not wood
Branching, upright coral form
expertClues: Violet tips are the key field mark — check fresh specimens
Soil-growing Ramaria species require careful identification

encounterRarity: Present in Ontario mixed forest; encountered occasionally in suitable habitat

images.attribution: (c) mycowalt, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by mycowalt

sourceNotes: Ramaria species challenging in Ontario. Violet tips help identify this species. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A large, richly golden-yellow branching coral from forest soil. One of the most colourful Ontario forest fungi.
descriptionLong: Golden Coral is one of the larger and more colourful Ramaria species in Ontario. The entire fruiting body is rich golden-yellow to egg-yolk yellow, with multiple upward-branching tips. The base is stout and white or pale. It grows from soil in deciduous and mixed forest in summer and fall. Ramaria species as a group are difficult to identify confidently at the species level — caution is needed as some are mildly toxic. The vivid golden-yellow colouration makes this species more identifiable than most.

Multiple branching tips all the same yellow colour
White to pale base
From soil in forest

Base is usually paler than the branches
No purple or violet tips — those indicate R. fumigata
confusionWarnings: Ramaria species require care — some are mildly toxic; do not eat without expert verification

encounterRarity: Encountered in suitable Ontario forest habitat; not abundant

images.attribution: (c) Amadej Trnkoczy, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA)

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario deciduous and mixed forest. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A bright yellow, brain-like blob of jelly on dead branches. Appears after rain and shrivels in dry weather.
descriptionLong: Witch's Butter is one of the most easily recognized jelly fungi: a bright, golden-yellow mass of brain-like, wavy, lobed jelly that grows on dead branches and twigs of hardwoods. It is rubbery and gelatinous when wet, shrivelling to a hard orange crust in dry conditions and rehydrating with rain. Technically, Tremella mesenterica is a parasite of other fungi (specifically Peniophora species) that live in the wood. Common throughout Ontario year-round. Not edible (little flavour or substance) but completely distinctive.

Gelatinous and rubbery when wet
Shrivels to hard orange crust when dry; rehydrates with rain
Year-round presence
expertClues: Shrivel-and-rehydrate behaviour is characteristic of jelly fungi
Technically parasitizes another fungus in the wood
On hardwood branches — not on conifers (which host a different yellow jelly)
confusionWarnings: Dacrymyces palmatus (orange jelly) is similar but more orange and grows on conifer wood
No dangerous lookalikes
encounterRarity: Extremely common year-round on dead hardwood branches across Ontario

images.attribution: (c) wojtest, all rights reserved, uploaded by wojtest

sourceNotes: One of the most common jelly fungi in Ontario. Year-round on dead hardwood. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A rubbery brown ear-shaped jelly fungus on dead wood — looks exactly like a small ear attached to a log. Edible and widely used in Asian cooking.
descriptionLong: Wood Ear is a jelly fungus that forms brown, rubbery, ear-shaped cups on dead hardwood. The inner surface (like the inside of an ear) is pale tan-brown and smooth; the outer surface is finely hairy and darker brown. When wet it is gelatinous and elastic; when dry it shrivels to a hard, dark, nearly black crust and rehydrates completely in water. Common on elder, maple, and other dead hardwoods. The cultivated form is used extensively in Asian cuisine (mu-er or cloud ear). The species in Ontario is Auricularia americana (previously considered the same as the European A. auricula-judae).
keyTraits: Brown ear-shaped rubbery cup on dead wood
Smooth pale inner surface; hairy darker outer surface
Gelatinous when wet; shrivels when dry
Rehydrates completely with water
expertClues: Ear shape is highly distinctive
Outer surface has fine grey-brown hairs
On hardwood — especially elder, maple
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — shape is unmistakeable
Rehydrate dried specimens in warm water for culinary use
encounterRarity: Found throughout Ontario on dead hardwood, especially after rain; year-round

images.attribution: (c) pinonbistro, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by pinonbistro

sourceNotes: Common year-round on Ontario dead hardwood. Edible and widely used. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A black, brain-like jelly blob on dead oak branches — like Witch's Butter but black. Appears after autumn and winter rains.
descriptionLong: Brown Witch's Butter (actually appearing black or very dark brown when wet) forms irregular, lobed, gelatinous blobs on dead oak branches and logs. The surface has small raised glands or warts. It is firm and rubbery when wet and shrivels to a nearly invisible dark crust in dry conditions. Common in autumn and winter on dead hardwood, particularly oak. Like Witch's Butter, it shrinks dramatically in dry conditions and completely rehydrates with rain. Not of any culinary interest.

Surface with small glands or warts
Shrivels in dry weather; rehydrates with rain
Autumn and winter fruiting
expertClues: Very dark colour (black) distinguishes it from yellow Witch's Butter
Small warts visible on the surface of fresh specimens
Oak substrate is typical
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes
Easy to miss when shrivelled in dry weather — look for dark flaking patches on oak branches
encounterRarity: Common on dead oak branches in Ontario in fall and winter; requires rain to be visible

images.attribution: (c) Denis Ivanov, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Denis Ivanov

sourceNotes: Common on Ontario dead oak. Winter and fall. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: Vivid orange cup-shaped discs on bare soil paths — look exactly like pieces of orange peel dropped on the ground.
descriptionLong: Orange Peel Fungus produces bright orange, irregular cup-shaped fruiting bodies directly on bare soil, gravel paths, and disturbed ground. The inner (upper) surface is vivid orange; the outer surface is pale and slightly hairy. The cups are thin, brittle, and irregular — often splitting or overlapping as they age. They are completely unmistakeable in Ontario: nothing else in bare path soil is that orange, that cup-shaped, and that size. Found in late summer through fall. Not of great culinary interest due to thin flesh.

Bright orange inner surface; pale outer surface
Grows on bare, disturbed soil and gravel paths
Thin and brittle

Often in clusters of multiple cups
Found on compacted bare soil — trail margins, campfire areas, road verges
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes given colour and substrate
Scarlet Cup (Sarcoscypha) is red-scarlet and grows on buried sticks in spring — different season and colour
encounterRarity: Very common on bare soil across Ontario in late summer and fall

images.attribution: (c) sea-kangaroo, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND), uploaded by sea-kangaroo

sourceNotes: Very common on bare disturbed soil across Ontario. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A brilliant scarlet-red cup fungus on buried sticks in early spring. One of the first fungi to appear after snowmelt.
descriptionLong: Scarlet Cup is one of the most beautiful — and most welcome — fungi in Ontario, because it appears in very early spring, often while snow is still melting. The cup is vivid scarlet-red inside (the spore-producing surface), with a pale pinkish to cream-white outer surface. Cups are up to 5 cm across, on short stems, often partially buried. They grow on small buried sticks, twigs, and woody debris in moist deciduous and mixed forest. Finding a cluster of bright red cups emerging from leaf litter and snow is one of spring's great fungal rewards.

Pale cream-white outer surface
Very early spring — often at snowmelt
On buried sticks in moist forest

Two species in Ontario (S. austriaca and S. coccinea) are nearly identical; both called Scarlet Cup
confusionWarnings: Orange Peel Fungus is orange and grows in summer on bare soil — different
No dangerous lookalikes
encounterRarity: A reward for early spring forest walks — not rare in good habitat but always memorable

images.attribution: (c) Björn Sothmann, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Björn Sothmann

sourceNotes: One of Ontario's earliest spring fungi. Appears at snowmelt in moist forest. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A medium to large brown cup or bowl fungus on soil and disturbed ground. Common and representative of the cup fungi group.
descriptionLong: Bay Cup is one of the most common brown cup fungi in Ontario. The fruiting body is a shallow to deep cup or bowl, dark bay-brown on the inner (upper) surface, paler and slightly mealy on the outer surface. Cups range from golf ball to fist-sized. They grow on soil — in gardens, disturbed ground, burnt areas, and sometimes forest humus. Peziza is a large and challenging genus with many similar species that require microscopy to confirm. Bay Cup is considered the most commonly encountered Ontario representative and a good introduction to the cup fungi.

Smooth inner surface; slightly mealy outer
Grows on soil — often disturbed ground
Brittle flesh
expertClues: Peziza species require microscopy for confident species-level ID
Bay Cup is a safe general-level ID for brown cup fungi in Ontario soil
Cups often split as they age
confusionWarnings: Many similar Peziza species — treat as a group ID unless microscopy available
No dangerous lookalikes
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario disturbed ground and forest humus throughout the growing season

images.attribution: (c) BiteYourBum.Com Photography, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

sourceNotes: Common Ontario cup fungus in disturbed soil. Genus Peziza requires microscopy for species confirmation. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A cascading waterfall of long white spines on dead or dying oaks. Unmistakeable — and one of Ontario's most celebrated edible fungi.
descriptionLong: Lion's Mane is one of the most spectacular fungi in Ontario. It produces a single, round to irregular mass of long, hanging, white to cream spines (up to 4 cm long) that cascade downward from a wound or hollow in a dead or dying hardwood — usually oak, beech, or maple. The mass can be 20–40 cm across. Young specimens are pure white; they yellow and orange with age. The spines are the spore-producing surface. It has no cap, stem, or gills. Lion's Mane has attracted enormous interest for its culinary value (flavour often compared to seafood or crab) and for research into nerve growth compounds.

Grows from wounds on hardwood trees
Pure white when young; yellows with age
Single unbranched mass (unlike Bear's Head)

Single, undivided clump distinguishes it from Bear's Head (branched)
Often high on living trees — look up
confusionWarnings: Bear's Head Tooth (Hericium americanum) is branched; Lion's Mane is a single clump — both edible
No dangerous lookalikes
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario hardwood forest — more frequent than it seems once you know to look up on old oaks

images.attribution: (c) rmillerwell, all rights reserved

sourceNotes: Present in southern Ontario on old hardwoods. Fall fruiting. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A branched, white tooth fungus hanging from dead hardwood — like a coral and a hedgehog combined. Related to Lion's Mane but with multiple branching clusters.
descriptionLong: Bear's Head Tooth is a close relative of Lion's Mane and equally spectacular. Instead of a single unbranched mass, it forms multiple branching arms, each ending in clusters of long, hanging, white spines. The overall structure is irregular and can be very large. It grows on dead hardwood — logs and stumps — often at ground level, unlike Lion's Mane which favours living trees. Like Lion's Mane, it is pure white when young, yellowing with age. Also considered an excellent edible. The branching structure distinguishes it from Lion's Mane.
keyTraits: Multiple branching arms each ending in clusters of hanging spines
White to cream — yellows with age
On dead hardwood logs and stumps
Branching structure (vs. single mass of Lion's Mane)
expertClues: Branching distinguishes it from Lion's Mane
On dead logs at ground level (vs. Lion's Mane on living trees aloft)
The two species sometimes hybridize — intermediate forms exist
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes
Lion's Mane is a single mass; Bear's Head is branched — both edible
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario hardwood forest on dead logs; not common but memorable

images.attribution: (c) chockman, all rights reserved

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario hardwood forest on dead logs. Branching distinguishes from Lion's Mane. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A cream to pale orange-tan mushroom with tiny spines (teeth) on the underside of its cap instead of gills or pores. A safe and reliable edible.
descriptionLong: The Hedgehog Mushroom is beloved by foragers because it is one of the easiest edible mushrooms to identify confidently: it looks like a regular creamy-tan cap-and-stem mushroom from above, but turn it over and the underside has densely packed short spines (teeth) instead of gills or pores. The cap is irregularly shaped, wavy, pale cream to orange-tan, with a downcurved margin. The stem is stout and pale. It grows from soil in mixed forest under oaks and beeches. No dangerous species has spines on the underside in Ontario, making this one of the safest beginners' finds.

Underside has densely packed tiny spines instead of gills or pores
Irregular, wavy cap — not a perfect circle
Grows from forest soil
expertClues: Spine underside is the single most important feature — check immediately
Flesh is firm and white; does not stain when cut
Often found in scattered groups under beech and oak
confusionWarnings: No dangerous Ontario species with spines on the cap underside — this feature alone is a safety marker
Hydnum umbilicatum is a smaller sister species with a navel-like depression in cap centre — also edible
encounterRarity: Regular in Ontario mixed forest in fall; an excellent find for any skill level

images.attribution: (c) Piotr Lukasik, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Piotr Lukasik

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario deciduous forest in fall. Spined underside is unique among Ontario cap-and-stem fungi. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: The most prized spring mushroom in Ontario — a honeycomb-pitted, hollow cap on a white stem. Appears for two weeks in May under old elms.
descriptionLong: The Yellow Morel is the most celebrated spring fungi find in Ontario. The cap is covered in a network of deep pits and ridges, creating a honeycombed or sponge-like surface. Young caps are grey and conical; mature ones become yellow-tan to ochre-brown. The cap is attached at the base to the hollow stem, and the entire mushroom — cap and stem — is hollow when sliced. They appear in late April to mid-May in Ontario, fruiting in mixed and hardwood forest, particularly under dying or recently dead elm, ash, and apple trees. The window is short — 1–2 weeks — making each find precious.

Entire mushroom hollow when cut lengthwise
Spring only — late April to mid-May in Ontario
Under dead or dying elm, ash, or apple
