sourceNotes: Classic Ontario spring fungi. Peak late April – mid May. Dead elm association is the key habitat clue. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A dark, conical morel that fruits on disturbed ground, burn sites, and wood chip beds — sometimes in large numbers. Darker and more conical than Yellow Morel.
descriptionLong: Black Morel typically fruits slightly earlier than Yellow Morel and often on more disturbed ground: burn sites (sometimes appearing in spectacular numbers the year after a forest fire), wood chip piles, mulched garden beds, and roadsides. The cap is conical to elongated, with a coarser honeycomb of pits and ridges that are dark brown to nearly black, contrasting with pale ridges. Like all true morels, the entire interior is hollow and the cap attaches at the base. The Morchella taxonomy has been revised extensively — M. importuna is currently the accepted name for this disturbed-ground black morel.
keyTraits: Dark brown to black pits in honeycomb pattern — paler ridges
Conical cap, attached at base to stem
Hollow throughout
Disturbed ground and burn sites
expertClues: Darker pits than Yellow Morel — more contrast between pit and ridge
Wood chips and disturbed soil are the best habitat clues
Sometimes massively abundant at burn sites 1–2 years post-fire
confusionWarnings: Same confusions as Yellow Morel — False Morel (Gyromitra) is brain-wrinkled, not honeycombed
Always cut in half to verify hollow interior
encounterRarity: Common at burn sites; present in disturbed ground across Ontario

images.attribution: (c) rmwintigard, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

sourceNotes: Disturbed-ground morel across Ontario. Burn sites and wood chip beds are key habitats. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); iNaturalist Ontario.

descriptionShort: A brain-like, saddle-shaped spring fungus that is toxic raw and dangerous even when cooked. Confused with true morels by beginners.
descriptionLong: The False Morel is a significant safety species in Ontario. It appears in spring at the same time as true morels, in similar habitats (often under conifers or in sandy soil near pine), and can be confused by beginners. The cap is not honeycombed like a true morel — instead it is irregularly lobed, brain-like, or saddle-shaped, with a wrinkled-rumpled surface. The stem is white and the cap may not be attached at the base. It contains gyromitrin, which is converted to monomethylhydrazine (rocket fuel) in the body — toxic even in many cooked preparations, and fatal in high doses. Not hollow throughout.
keyTraits: Brain-like, lobed, saddle-shaped cap — NOT a honeycomb
Cap wrinkled and rumpled, not pitted
Spring fruiting near conifers and in sandy soil
Not fully hollow — chambered or partially filled
expertClues: Cap surface is wrinkled-lobed, not pitted — key distinction from true morels
Interior is chambered, not simply hollow
Often under pine or in sandy disturbed soil near conifers
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — gyromitrin causes liver and blood damage; can be fatal
NEVER eat any spring fungus without confirming it is a true morel (honeycomb pitted, fully hollow, cap attached at base)
Cooking reduces but does not eliminate the toxin
encounterRarity: Present across Ontario in spring near conifers; less common than true morels

images.attribution: (c) Kari Pihlaviita, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

sourceNotes: Important safety species for spring morel season in Ontario. Pine association is a habitat clue. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); North American Mycological Association.

descriptionShort: An unmistakeable phallic fungus with a white spongy stalk and a dark olive cap covered in foul-smelling spore slime. Emerges from an egg in garden mulch.
descriptionLong: The Common Stinkhorn is one of the most remarkable fungi in Ontario — unmistakeable in form and unforgettable in smell. The mature fruiting body is tall, white, and spongy with a thimble-shaped cap coated in dark olive-brown slime (gleba) that smells powerfully of rotting flesh or sewage. This smell attracts flies, which disperse the spores. The base emerges from a round white egg-like structure buried in soil or mulch. The 'egg' stage is firm and white outside, with a jelly-like interior encasing the compressed stalk. Found in garden beds, wood chip mulch, and forest edges.
keyTraits: Tall white spongy stalk with olive slime-covered cap
Powerful foul smell of rotting meat
Emerges from a white egg buried in mulch or soil
Attracts flies
expertClues: Egg stage is firm with jelly interior — distinct from puffballs by the figure inside when cross-sectioned
Full adult appears overnight when conditions are right
Smell detectable from metres away
confusionWarnings: No other Ontario species shares this combination of features
Egg stage is distinctive on cross-section — shows the compressed stalkhead inside
encounterRarity: Regular in garden mulch and wood chip beds across Ontario; often a surprise to homeowners

images.attribution: (c) Nick Weller, all rights reserved, uploaded by Nick Weller

sourceNotes: Common in Ontario garden mulch beds. Egg stage is distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A slender, orange-tipped stinkhorn — smaller and more delicate than the Common Stinkhorn, but equally distinctive. Found in garden beds and forest litter.
descriptionLong: The Dog Stinkhorn is a slender relative of the Common Stinkhorn, smaller in stature and without a separate cap structure. The mature fruiting body is a narrow, tapering, pinkish-white to orange-pink spongy stalk with an orange-red tip covered in dark olive-brown spore slime. Like its larger relative, it smells powerfully of rotting matter and attracts flies for spore dispersal. It emerges from a small white egg buried in soil, forest humus, or garden beds. Not nearly as dramatic as the Common Stinkhorn but equally identifiable.
keyTraits: Slender pinkish-white spongy stalk tapering to an orange-red tip
Olive-brown spore slime on the tip
Foul smell — attracts flies
Emerges from a small egg in soil
expertClues: Smaller and more slender than Common Stinkhorn — no separate thimble-shaped cap
Orange tip is characteristic
Found in shadier, more forested settings than Common Stinkhorn
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes
Smaller and less dramatic than Common Stinkhorn; beginners may not recognize it as a stinkhorn at first
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario garden beds and forest humus; easy to overlook due to small size

images.attribution: (c) Nicolas Schwab, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Nicolas Schwab

sourceNotes: Common in Ontario gardens and forest humus. Slender form distinguishes it from Common Stinkhorn. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: Thin, overlapping fan-shaped brackets on dead logs with concentric zones of yellow, orange, and grey — and a smooth orange-brown underside. Often mistaken for Turkey Tail.
descriptionLong: Hairy Curtain Crust is one of Ontario's most common wood fungi, often growing in the same habitat as Turkey Tail and frequently mistaken for it by beginners. The caps are thin, fan-shaped, and grow in overlapping rows. The top is hairy (hirsute) with concentric zones of yellow, orange, tan, and grey. The key difference from Turkey Tail: the underside is smooth and orange-brown — not pored. Turkey Tail has a fine pored white underside; Stereum has a smooth non-pored underside. Learning this distinction is a foundational skill for Ontario naturalists.
keyTraits: Thin fan-shaped overlapping brackets with yellow-orange-grey zones
Smooth orange-brown underside — NO pores
Hairy (velvety) top surface
Year-round on dead hardwood
expertClues: Touch the underside — smooth means Stereum, pores mean Turkey Tail or similar polypore
Underside may bleed red when scratched on fresh specimens of some Stereum species
Often impossible to pull off the log cleanly — attached firmly
confusionWarnings: Commonly confused with Turkey Tail — check for pores on the underside
Turkey Tail has white to cream pored underside; Hairy Curtain Crust has smooth orange-brown underside
encounterRarity: Extremely common on dead hardwood across Ontario year-round

images.attribution: (c) Natalie Gavidia, all rights reserved, uploaded by Natalie Gavidia

sourceNotes: One of Ontario's most common wood fungi. The smooth underside vs. Turkey Tail pores is a key teaching point. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A cream to pale orange crust with a wrinkled, vein-like pore surface. Forms flat, irregular patches on the undersides of dead hardwood logs.
descriptionLong: Bleeding Broadleaf Crust is a common but overlooked resupinate (crust-forming) fungus found on the underside of dead hardwood logs and branches. The fruiting body is flat and crust-like with a cream to pale orange surface covered in shallow, irregular wrinkles, pores, or veins. The margin is often white or cottony and frayed. It causes white rot in hardwoods. Resupinate fungi as a group are among the most species-rich in Ontario but are rarely noticed — they grow pressed flat against wood and often look like peeling paint or dried foam.
keyTraits: Flat crust pressed directly against wood — no cap
Cream to pale orange with wrinkled or vein-like surface
White cottony margin
On underside of dead hardwood logs
expertClues: Look under logs and branches — resupinate fungi grow where they're protected from rain
The vein-like pore surface is characteristic of Byssomerulius
Identifies the broad category of resupinate fungi for beginners
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — resupinate fungi are not eaten
Many similar resupinate species exist — treat as genus or family level for casual observation
encounterRarity: Extremely common on dead Ontario hardwood — easy to overlook without flipping logs

images.attribution: (c) Cindi Fitzgerald, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Cindi Fitzgerald

sourceNotes: Common resupinate fungus on Ontario dead hardwood. Introduces crust fungi to young naturalists. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: Hard black finger-like clubs growing from buried wood and stumps. Looks exactly like charred fingers or toes emerging from the ground.
descriptionLong: Dead Man's Fingers is one of Ontario's most immediately striking fungi — once seen, never forgotten. It produces hard, irregular, finger-like clubs 3–8 cm tall, black on the outside (from embedded spores), with a white to grey interior. They typically appear as a cluster of 3–6 fingers emerging from a buried stump or from the ground near rotting hardwood. Young specimens may be pale grey or cream and powdery before blackening. They are an ascomycete — spores form in tiny flasks (perithecia) embedded in the surface. Completely harmless, but unfailingly startling in appearance.
keyTraits: Hard black finger-like clubs from buried wood or stumps
3–6 fingers in a cluster
Black outer surface; white interior
Year-round — long-lasting structures
expertClues: Year-round presence — the hard black clubs persist for months
Young specimens are grey or cream; only become black when spores mature
Perithecia (spore flasks) embedded in surface — visible as tiny dots
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — appearance is unique
Candlesnuff fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon) is related but much smaller and white-tipped
encounterRarity: Extremely common on buried hardwood stumps across Ontario; year-round

images.attribution: (c) Maria Romanova, all rights reserved, uploaded by Maria Romanova

sourceNotes: Ubiquitous on Ontario buried hardwood. Year-round. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A tiny, branching black stalk with white powdery tips on rotting wood — looks like miniature antlers or a snuffed candle.
descriptionLong: Candlesnuff is a small but distinctive ascomycete that grows on rotting hardwood logs and stumps throughout Ontario. The fruiting bodies are branched, thin, antler-like structures 1–4 cm tall — black at the base and white to grey at the tips from a coating of asexual spores (conidia). Later in the season the entire structure darkens and the sexual spores are produced in tiny embedded flasks. They grow in clusters from dead wood, particularly in autumn and winter. While tiny, they are highly photogenic and easy to spot with close attention.
keyTraits: Tiny branched antler-like structures on rotting wood
White to grey powdery tips; black base
1–4 cm tall; in clusters
Fall through winter fruiting
expertClues: White tips are from asexual spores — rub gently and white powder transfers
Related to Dead Man's Fingers — same genus
Often overlooked due to small size
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — size and form are unique
Dead Man's Fingers is larger and fully black
encounterRarity: Very common on rotting Ontario hardwood in fall and winter; requires looking closely

images.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Stephen James McWilliam

sourceNotes: Common on Ontario dead hardwood. Distinctive white-tipped antler form. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A puffball encased in a star-shaped outer skin that splits and peels back to form 4–8 brown rays — like a star on the forest floor.
descriptionLong: Earthstars are one of Ontario's most extraordinary fungi. They begin as a round, brown structure like a small puffball. As they mature, the outer skin splits from the top and peels back into 4–8 pointed rays (like a star), raising the inner puffball sac off the ground. The rays curl back and may even fold under the sac in dry conditions to help anchor it. The inner sac is pale brown and papery, with a small pore at the top through which spores are released. Geastrum saccatum is the most commonly encountered earthstar in Ontario — found in forest humus and leaf litter.
keyTraits: Star-shaped outer skin split into 4–8 rays
Central round papery sac with small apical pore
Rays can curl under the sac in dry conditions
Forest floor in leaf litter
expertClues: Multiple Geastrum species in Ontario — G. saccatum is most common and has a distinct collar around pore
Rays do not absorb water (hygroscopic) in G. saccatum — rays stay splayed
Size typically 2–4 cm including rays
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — the star form is unmistakeable
Different Geastrum species require attention to pore collar, ray texture, and hygroscopic behaviour
encounterRarity: Present but not abundant in Ontario forest — a genuinely exciting find

images.attribution: (c) JJ Harrison, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

sourceNotes: Most common earthstar in Ontario forest. Star form is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A tiny white to grey fan-shaped bracket on dead wood — with bizarre split gills that close in dry conditions and reopen in rain. One of the world's most widespread fungi.
descriptionLong: Splitgill is possibly the most widespread and common wood fungus in the world — found on every continent except Antarctica. In Ontario, it grows year-round on dead twigs and logs of hardwoods. The cap is tiny (1–4 cm), fan-shaped, hairy, white to grey. The underside appears to have gills, but they are unique: each 'gill' is actually split down the centre, curling apart in two halves. In dry conditions the gills curl closed and the entire fungus shrivels; in rain, they reopen and sporulate. This drying-rehydrating cycle can repeat hundreds of times. Not edible (tough), but fascinating.
keyTraits: Tiny white to grey fan-shaped bracket on dead wood
Gills split in two along their length — unique
Shrivels in dry conditions; rehydrates in rain
Year-round; on small dead branches and twigs
expertClues: Use a hand lens to see the split gills clearly
One of the world's most studied fungi — genome sequenced early
Found on very small wood pieces — even door frames in old buildings
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — split gills are unique
Small size and pale colour make it easily overlooked
encounterRarity: One of the most common fungi in the world — present on dead wood year-round across Ontario

images.attribution: (c) Grey Smith, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Grey Smith

sourceNotes: Global species — extremely common in Ontario on dead wood year-round. Split gills are unique. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A small, yellow-stemmed funnel mushroom with a hollow stem and ridged underside. Grows in late fall in mossy conifer forest, often in large troops.
descriptionLong: The Funnel Chanterelle (Yellowfoot) is a close relative of the Black Trumpet and a more distant relative of the Golden Chanterelle. The cap is brown to grey-brown, thin, irregular, and funnelled with a wavy edge. The underside has blunt, forked, grey-blue ridges running down the stem (like chanterelle, not true gills). The stem is hollow and distinctly yellow — giving rise to the name 'Yellowfoot.' It grows in large troops in late fall, often in mossy ground in conifer or mixed forest. One of Ontario's latest mushrooms of the season. Edible and good.
keyTraits: Grey-brown funnel-shaped cap with wavy edge
Hollow yellow stem — 'Yellowfoot'
Blunt forked grey ridges on underside (not gills)
Late fall in moss under conifers
expertClues: Yellow hollow stem is the primary field mark
Grows in troops — finding one means many are nearby
Late fall timing: October–November in Ontario, after most other mushrooms are done
confusionWarnings: Not easily confused with dangerous species given the yellow hollow stem
Distinguished from Golden Chanterelle by brown cap, yellow stem, hollow stem, and late season
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario conifer and mixed forest in late fall; found in good numbers in the right mossy habitat

images.attribution: (c) growbadlands, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by growbadlands

sourceNotes: Late fall find in Ontario conifer forest. Yellow hollow stem is distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: Dense clusters of honey-coloured caps on conifer stumps, with smoky grey-lilac gills. An edible and very common late-season find.
descriptionLong: Conifer Tuft is one of the most common autumn mushrooms in Ontario, growing in large, dense clusters on conifer stumps and logs — particularly spruce and pine. The cap is honey-yellow to pale orange-brown, smooth, paler at the margins. The gills are an unusual smoky grey to lilac-grey colour, distinguishing this species from the similar Sulphur Tuft (Hypholoma fasciculare) which has vivid yellow gills and is toxic. Flesh is pale; taste mild to slightly bitter. The grey gills and conifer substrate together are the key identifiers. Considered a good edible when thoroughly cooked.
keyTraits: Honey-yellow to orange-brown caps in dense clusters
Smoky grey to lilac-grey gills — NOT yellow
On conifer stumps and logs
Pale flesh; mild taste
expertClues: Grey gills are the most important distinguishing feature from toxic Sulphur Tuft
Conifer substrate confirms identity — Sulphur Tuft grows on both hardwood and conifer
Purple-brown spore print

Galerina marginata also clusters on wood and has a ring; it is deadly — check for ring on stem and spore print colour
encounterRarity: Very common on conifer stumps across Ontario in fall — often found in impressive clusters

images.attribution: (c) Иван Матершев, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Иван Матершев

sourceNotes: Common fall find on Ontario conifer stumps. Grey gills distinguish it from toxic Sulphur Tuft. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A small, brown, ringed mushroom that clusters on wood — nearly identical to Honey Mushroom and Conifer Tuft, but deadly. The most dangerous look-alike in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Deadly Galerina is the most dangerous look-alike mushroom in Ontario because it grows in the same habitat and at the same time as commonly harvested edibles: Honey Mushroom (Armillaria), Conifer Tuft (Hypholoma capnoides), and Velvet Shank (Flammulina). The cap is honey-brown to tawny, smooth, hygrophanous — darkening from the centre outward as it dries. It has a small but distinct ring on the stem. The gills are rusty-brown from the spores. It grows in clusters or singly on rotting wood — both conifer and hardwood. Contains amatoxins identical to the Destroying Angel. A single cap mixed into a harvest of Honey Mushrooms can be fatal.
keyTraits: Small honey-brown cap — smooth, dries paler from centre outward
Ring on stem — small but present
Rusty-brown gills and spore print
Clusters on rotting wood
expertClues: Spore print is rusty-brown — the single most important test; Honey Mushroom gives white print
Ring is often fragile and may disappear on older specimens
Never harvest small brown ringed mushrooms from wood without a spore print
confusionWarnings: DEADLY — amatoxins cause fatal liver failure
Almost indistinguishable from Honey Mushroom, Conifer Tuft, and Velvet Shank by appearance alone
Spore print test is mandatory: rusty-brown = Galerina, white = Honey Mushroom, purple-brown = Conifer Tuft
encounterRarity: Common on rotting wood across Ontario in fall — present wherever its look-alikes grow

images.attribution: (c) Fluff Berger, all rights reserved

sourceNotes: Critical safety species across Ontario. Co-occurs with harvested edibles. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); North American Mycological Association.

descriptionShort: Dense clusters of sulphur-yellow caps on stumps and logs with vivid yellow gills. Toxic — the critical comparison species for the edible Conifer Tuft.
descriptionLong: Sulphur Tuft is one of the most common toxic mushrooms in Ontario, growing in large dense clusters on dead hardwood and conifer stumps year-round. The cap is sulphur-yellow to orange-yellow, sometimes with a darker olive-brown centre. The gills are vivid yellow becoming greenish-grey as the dark purple-brown spores mature — this gill colour is the key distinguishing feature from the edible Conifer Tuft, which has smoky grey gills. The taste is extremely bitter. Toxic: causes severe gastrointestinal illness. It is so common that it is arguably more important to know than many rarer toxic species.

Vivid yellow to greenish-yellow gills — NOT grey
Dense clusters on stumps and logs
Extremely bitter taste
expertClues: Gill colour is the key: yellow-green in Sulphur Tuft vs. smoky grey in edible Conifer Tuft
Bitter taste is detectable even in a tiny piece — do not swallow
Grows on both hardwood and conifer; Conifer Tuft is conifer-only
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — causes severe gastrointestinal illness
Most dangerously confused with Conifer Tuft (Hypholoma capnoides) — check gill colour carefully
Also confused with Honey Mushroom and other clustered wood species
encounterRarity: Extremely common on dead wood across Ontario from spring through fall

images.attribution: (c) Nicolas Schwab, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Nicolas Schwab

sourceNotes: One of the most common toxic species on Ontario dead wood. Gill colour vs. Conifer Tuft is the key safety lesson. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A vivid orange funnel mushroom with thin crowded forking gills. Looks like a chanterelle but has true blade-like gills. Mildly toxic.
descriptionLong: False Chanterelle illustrates the key difference between a true chanterelle and its look-alikes. The cap is vivid orange, funnel-shaped, with a strongly inrolled margin. But the underside has thin, crowded, repeatedly forking, blade-like gills — not the thick, blunt, forked ridges of a true chanterelle. The flesh is orange throughout, not white when cut. It grows on soil or mossy ground near woody debris and conifers. Mildly toxic in most people, causing gastrointestinal upset; potentially more serious in some individuals.
keyTraits: Vivid orange funnel cap with strongly inrolled edge
Thin, crowded, blade-like gills — not blunt forked ridges
Orange flesh throughout when cut — not white
Usually near decaying conifer wood
expertClues: Run a fingernail across the underside — thin sharp blades vs. thick blunt ridges
Flesh is orange; chanterelle flesh is white to pale yellow
Inrolled margin more extreme and persistent than on chanterelle
confusionWarnings: Mildly to moderately toxic — causes gastrointestinal illness
Primary look-alike for Golden Chanterelle — always check gill type and flesh colour
Jack-o'-Lantern is more toxic and clusters from buried wood
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario conifer forest in fall; often found near true chanterelles

images.attribution: (c) Matthieu Morin, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Matthieu Morin

sourceNotes: Common Ontario fall species. The primary comparison species for chanterelle identification. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A large impressive parasol mushroom in lawns — white with brown scales and gills that turn green. The most common cause of mushroom poisoning in North America.
descriptionLong: Green-spored Parasol is the single most common cause of mushroom poisoning in North America, responsible for more emergency room visits than any other species. It grows in lawns, parks, and grassy areas — often in fairy rings — and looks impressively edible: a large white parasol with brown scales, white gills, and a ring on the stem. The critical identifier: the gills start white but turn green as the spores mature. Any white parasol mushroom in a lawn with even a faint greenish tint to the gills should be avoided. Severe gastrointestinal illness — violent vomiting — begins within 1–3 hours of eating.

Gills start white but turn distinctly green as spores mature
Double ring on stem
In lawns and grassy areas — often in rings or arcs
expertClues: Green spore print is absolutely diagnostic
Young gills may still be white — always check for faint green tint or take a spore print
Cap can be very large — 15–30 cm — impressive and tempting to beginners
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — most common cause of mushroom poisoning in North America; causes violent vomiting
White gills in young specimens look identical to edible parasols — always check for green
Confused with Shaggy Parasol (Chlorophyllum rhacodes) which has white spore print
encounterRarity: Regular in southern Ontario lawns and parks in summer and fall, especially in warmer years

images.attribution: (c) Christian Schwarz, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Christian Schwarz

sourceNotes: #1 cause of mushroom poisoning ER visits in North America. Increasingly common in southern Ontario. Reference: North American Mycological Association; MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A large shaggy-scaled parasol whose white flesh stains orange-red when cut. Found in gardens, parks, and compost. Edible for most but causes reactions in some.
descriptionLong: Shaggy Parasol is a large, handsome mushroom with a white cap covered in coarse, upturned brown scales. The flesh is white but stains orange then red-brown when cut or bruised — a distinctive and important feature. It has a large double-edged ring that slides freely up and down the smooth stem. The stem base is distinctly bulbous. Found in gardens, compost heaps, wood chip beds, and forest edges. Generally considered edible when thoroughly cooked, but it causes gastrointestinal reactions in a significant proportion of people.
keyTraits: Large white cap with coarse shaggy brown scales
Flesh stains orange then red-brown when cut
Large double ring that slides freely on smooth stem
Bulbous stem base
expertClues: Reddening flesh is the key diagnostic feature
Ring slides freely — not fixed
Grows in disturbed, nutrient-rich ground
confusionWarnings: Can cause gastrointestinal reactions even when cooked — individual sensitivity varies
Confused with Green-spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites) — check for any green tint in gills
White spore print confirms it is not Green-spored Parasol
encounterRarity: Regular in Ontario gardens and parks from summer through fall

images.attribution: (c) Kenton Kwok, all rights reserved, uploaded by Kenton Kwok

sourceNotes: Common in Ontario gardens and compost. Reddening flesh is key. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A button mushroom look-alike that turns vivid chrome-yellow at the stem base when cut and smells of ink or phenol. Toxic.
descriptionLong: Yellow Stainer looks nearly identical to the edible Meadow Mushroom — white cap, pink-then-brown gills, ring on stem, in lawns. The difference: when the base of the stem is cut or scraped, it immediately turns vivid chrome-yellow. The cap edge may also yellow when damaged. The smell is chemical — like ink, phenol, or a hospital ward. Toxic: causes severe gastrointestinal illness in most people within 30 minutes of eating, faster than most toxic species. The cut-stem test is the most important routine check for any white lawn Agaricus.

Stem base turns vivid chrome-yellow immediately when cut
Smells of ink, phenol, or chemicals
Pink to brown gills; ring on stem

Phenol smell is reliable and distinctive — off-putting rather than pleasant
Cap edge may also yellow when rubbed or damaged
confusionWarnings: TOXIC — causes rapid severe gastrointestinal illness
Indistinguishable from Meadow Mushroom and Horse Mushroom without the cut test
Always cut the stem base of any white lawn Agaricus before collecting
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario lawns and parks alongside edible Agaricus species

images.attribution: (c) Damon Tighe, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Damon Tighe

sourceNotes: Very common alongside edible Agaricus in Ontario. The cut-base yellow-stain test is a critical field skill. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A large white Agaricus with an anise-like smell and a cog-wheel patterned ring. Found in fields and pastures. Edible and good.
descriptionLong: Horse Mushroom is one of Ontario's finest field mushrooms — large, robust, white to off-white, with a pleasantly anise-like or almond smell. The cap can be 10–20 cm across. Gills start pale pink then darken to chocolate-brown. The ring is double-edged with a distinctive gear-tooth or cog-wheel pattern on its underside. Flesh is white and only very slightly yellows at the cap skin — not the vivid chrome-yellow of the Yellow Stainer. Found in open fields, pastures, and grassy woodland edges. The anise smell is the best quick identifier.
keyTraits: Large white to off-white cap
Anise or almond smell — pleasant and distinctive
Cog-wheel pattern on underside of ring
Pink gills darkening to chocolate-brown
expertClues: Anise smell is the primary field test — distinctive and consistent
Ring has gear-tooth pattern on its lower surface
Flesh may slightly yellow at cap skin but NOT vivid chrome-yellow at stem base
confusionWarnings: Always perform the cut-stem-base test: no vivid chrome-yellow = not Yellow Stainer

encounterRarity: Present in Ontario fields and meadows in summer and fall; less common than Meadow Mushroom

images.attribution: (c) Felipe Hidalgo, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Felipe Hidalgo

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario meadows and pastures. Anise smell and cog-wheel ring are key identifiers. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Audubon Society Field Guide.

descriptionShort: A small vivid violet-purple mushroom with thick spaced gills. One of the most colourful common mushrooms in the Ontario forest.
descriptionLong: The Amethyst Deceiver is small but strikingly beautiful — vivid violet-purple throughout: cap, gills, and stem. The cap is convex to flat, often with a depressed centre, and fades dramatically as it dries, from rich purple to pale lavender or buff. The gills are thick, widely spaced, and violet-purple. The stem is slender and fibrous. It grows from soil under beech, oak, and other hardwoods in small groups. The 'Deceiver' name comes from its extreme colour variation with age and dryness — fresh specimens are unmistakeable; faded ones can look like a completely different species.

Thick, widely spaced, purple gills
Fades dramatically to buff with age or dryness
Small — rarely more than 4 cm cap

images.attribution: (c) Christian Berg, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Christian Berg

sourceNotes: Common in Ontario deciduous forest. Colour is unmistakeable when fresh. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A small pinkish-tan mushroom with thick widely-spaced gills. Extremely common and extremely variable — it looks different every time you find it.
descriptionLong: The Common Deceiver earns its name through extreme variability: depending on moisture, age, and light, the same species can appear pinkish-tan, brick-red, watery brown, pale ochre, or almost white. The cap is convex to flat with a slightly wavy edge, the centre often slightly depressed. The gills are the constant: thick, widely spaced, and pink to flesh-coloured. The stem is fibrous and matches the cap. White spore print. It grows in enormous numbers in all types of Ontario forest throughout the season. The combination of habitat, thick spaced gills, and pinkish tones is consistent even when the overall colour is not.
keyTraits: Pinkish to brick-red to tan — highly variable colour
Thick, widely-spaced, pink gills — consistent across all forms
Fibrous stem matching cap colour
Grows in large numbers across forest floor
expertClues: Thick widely-spaced gills are the most consistent identifier despite colour variation
White spore print
One of the most common forest fungi — present in virtually every Ontario wood
confusionWarnings: Colour variability is extreme — do not rely on colour alone
No dangerous lookalikes when gill spacing and pinkish tone are confirmed
encounterRarity: Among the most abundant small mushrooms in Ontario forests

images.attribution: (c) Christian Schwarz, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Christian Schwarz

sourceNotes: Among the most common small mushrooms in Ontario. Classic introduction to colour-variable species. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A bright scarlet-red Russula with a white stem and white brittle gills. One acrid nibble will confirm this is not edible — and introduces the important Russula genus.
descriptionLong: The Sickener is bright scarlet — sometimes fading to pink at the margin — with a smooth shiny cap sticky when wet. The stem is pure white, brittle, and snaps cleanly like chalk. The gills are white, brittle, widely-spaced, and produce no milk when broken. The flesh is white with a red layer just under the cap skin. The taste is very acrid — a hot, peppery burn. This acrid taste is diagnostic and is the safety test: spit it out immediately. All Russula species have this brittle, chalk-snapping flesh due to their unique cell structure (sphaerocysts). The Sickener is the gateway species for learning this large and important genus.
keyTraits: Bright scarlet to red cap — clean and shiny
Pure white stem and gills — brittle like chalk, snaps cleanly
Very acrid, burning taste
No milk when gills broken — distinguishes from Lactarius
expertClues: Chalk-snap of the stem is characteristic of all Russula
Tiny taste test (spit immediately): acrid burn confirms R. emetica group
Cap skin peels easily from the edge inward — another Russula feature
confusionWarnings: Toxic — causes vomiting; acrid taste usually prevents accidental ingestion
Several red-capped Russula species exist; most are acrid and should be avoided by beginners
No deadly lookalikes within Russula in Ontario
encounterRarity: Very common in conifer and mixed forest across Ontario in summer and fall

images.attribution: (c) noah_siegel, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by noah_siegel

sourceNotes: Common Ontario species; introduces the Russula genus. Brittle flesh and acrid taste are genus-level features. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A striking Russula with a pale green cap that cracks and breaks into a mosaic of patches. One of the most reliably identified and prized edible Russulas.
descriptionLong: Green Cracking Russula is one of the most distinctive Russula species and a favourite of foragers because its appearance is unmistakeable: the cap is pale green to sage-green with a surface that cracks and breaks into a patch-work or tile-like mosaic as it matures, creating a unique texture. The stem and gills are pure white and brittle. The flesh is firm, white, and mild in taste — not acrid. Found under oak and beech in deciduous forest. It is considered one of the best edible Russulas and is used extensively in European cuisine.
keyTraits: Pale green to sage-green cap with cracked mosaic or tile-like surface
White brittle stem and gills
Mild, non-acrid taste — pleasant
Under oak and beech
expertClues: Cracked patchwork cap surface is highly distinctive — no other Ontario species looks like this
Mild taste confirms edibility within the Russula framework
Firm dense flesh — cuts cleanly
confusionWarnings: Green Russula species require care — some toxic green fungi exist but the cracked cap surface is unique
Confirm mild (not acrid) taste before use
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario deciduous forest under oak and beech; a rewarding find

images.attribution: (c) Ján Červenka, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Ján Červenka

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario oak and beech forest. Cracked green cap is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A wine-red to purple-brown Russula with a distinctive crab or shrimp smell when handled. One of the most common large Russulas in Ontario conifer forest.
descriptionLong: Crab Brittlegill is a medium to large Russula with a wine-red to purple-red or brownish-purple cap. The stem is flushed with pink or red. The gills are cream-coloured, darkening to ochre. The key identifying feature is the smell: when handled, rubbed, or bruised, it gives off a distinctive crab, shrimp, or fishy seafood smell that becomes stronger when dried. This smell is unusual and memorable. Found under pine, spruce, and other conifers in summer and fall. The seafood smell makes it one of the more confidently identifiable Russulas.
keyTraits: Wine-red to purple-red cap
Smell of crab or shrimp — distinctive and persistent
Cream to ochre gills; brittle white stem flushed pink or red
Under conifers
expertClues: Smell intensifies on drying and with age — crush a small piece
Stem flushed with red or pink is a consistent feature
Iron sulphate (FeSO4) test: flesh turns greenish — useful for confirmation
confusionWarnings: Several wine-red Russula species exist — seafood smell distinguishes this one
Mild to pleasant taste — not acrid
encounterRarity: Common in Ontario conifer forest; one of the most frequently encountered large Russulas

images.attribution: (c) Uwe Winkler, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Uwe Winkler

sourceNotes: Common in Ontario conifer forest. Seafood smell is the key field identifier. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A vivid orange-capped mushroom under pine that bleeds bright orange-saffron milk when cut. One of Ontario's most prized edible species.
descriptionLong: Saffron Milk Cap is one of the most distinctive and prized edible fungi in Ontario. The cap is orange to orange-salmon, with faint concentric zones, smooth and slightly sticky when wet, developing green staining with age. The gills are crowded and also orange. When any part is cut or broken, it exudes bright orange to saffron-coloured latex (milk) that does not change colour quickly. The stem is orange with orange-coloured pits. It grows from soil under pine — it is a strict mycorrhizal partner of pines. The combination of orange cap, orange gills, and orange milk under pine is essentially diagnostic.
keyTraits: Orange to salmon cap with faint concentric zones
Bright orange-saffron milk (latex) when cut
Orange gills — crowded and narrow
Strictly under pine
expertClues: Orange milk is the single most diagnostic feature
Cap and flesh develop green staining with age — distinctive and not present in other Ontario milk caps
Orange pits on the stem surface are characteristic
confusionWarnings: The Lactarius species group in Ontario includes L. deliciosus and L. deterrimus — both are similar and edible
False Saffron Milk Cap (Lactarius deterrimus) has milk that slowly turns wine-red — also edible but check
encounterRarity: Present wherever pine grows in Ontario — reliable in pine plantations and native pine forest in fall

images.attribution: (c) timendez, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by timendez

sourceNotes: Prized edible in Ontario pine forest. Orange milk is diagnostic. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A large, dark olive-brown mushroom under birch with white milk that turns yellow on a handkerchief. Very common but very unattractive — and mildly toxic.
descriptionLong: Birch Milk Cap is one of the most common and distinctive Lactarius species in Ontario, abundant wherever birch grows. The cap is dark olive-brown to sooty dark brown, convex with an inrolled margin, sticky when wet. The gills are cream-coloured, staining brown when damaged. When any part is broken, it exudes copious white milk — and this milk turns yellow on white paper or a handkerchief within minutes. It grows under birch — a strict mycorrhizal partner. Mildly toxic when raw and generally considered inedible or of very poor quality.
keyTraits: Dark olive-brown to sooty cap — large and unattractive
White milk that turns yellow on white paper within minutes
Under birch — strictly associated
Cream gills staining brown when damaged
expertClues: Yellow-staining milk test: break a gill and dab on white tissue — yellow within 5 minutes
Habitat under birch is the first clue
One of the largest and most common Lactarius in Ontario birch forest
confusionWarnings: Mildly toxic when raw; not recommended for eating
Dark colour and large size distinguish it from most other Ontario milk caps
encounterRarity: Very common in Ontario birch forest and mixed birch woodland from summer through fall

images.attribution: (c) Christian Schwarz, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Christian Schwarz

sourceNotes: Very common under Ontario birch. Yellow-staining milk is diagnostic. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A golden-yellow mushroom densely covered in shaggy upturned scales on both cap and stem. Grows in clusters at the base of trees. Distinctive and dramatic.
descriptionLong: Scaly Pholiota is one of Ontario's most visually distinctive wood-base mushrooms. The entire cap and stem are covered in dense, upward-curling, dry scales — the cap is golden-yellow to tawny underneath the scales, and the stem is the same colour, becoming darker toward the base. Gills are pale yellow, crowding the stem, darkening to rusty brown as the spores mature. It grows in dense clusters at the base of living or dead hardwoods — especially birch, maple, and aspen — from late summer through fall. While technically in the edible literature, it tastes bitter and can cause gastrointestinal reactions; better to leave it.
keyTraits: Dense upturned dry scales on both cap and stem
Golden-yellow to tawny background colour
Clusters at tree base
Gills start pale then darken to rusty brown
expertClues: Scales on the stem — not just the cap — is the key field mark
Rusty-brown spore print
Can be confused with Honey Mushroom from a distance; check for scales and bitter taste
confusionWarnings: Can cause gastrointestinal reactions; bitter taste; not recommended for eating
Confused with Honey Mushroom at tree base — Honey Mushroom has smooth cap, not scaly
encounterRarity: Common at the base of hardwoods across Ontario in fall

images.attribution: (c) Алексей Эбель, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Алексей Эбель

sourceNotes: Common at hardwood base in Ontario. Dense scaly covering of cap and stem is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A tawny-brown Cortinarius with a cobweb veil and rusty gills. Contains orellanine — a kidney-destroying toxin whose symptoms are delayed 2–3 weeks. Introduces the Cortinarius genus.
descriptionLong: Deadly Webcap is one of the most insidious toxic mushrooms in Ontario because its orellanine toxin causes kidney failure with symptoms delayed 2 to 3 weeks after eating — long after the mushroom is forgotten. The cap is tawny-orange to brick-brown, dry and fibrous. The gills are rusty-brown from the spores. The most important Cortinarius feature: the cobweb-like cortina — a fine, silky, spider-web veil connecting the cap edge to the stem — which leaves rusty-brown fibres on the stem as it tears. Found under conifers, particularly spruce. Responsible for fatalities in Scandinavia and present in Ontario; not abundant but important to know.
keyTraits: Tawny-orange to brick-brown fibrous cap
Cobweb-like cortina veil — leaves rusty fibres on stem
Rusty-brown gills from spores
Under conifers — particularly spruce
expertClues: Cortina (cobweb veil) is the key genus feature — look for rusty fibres on the stem
Rusty-brown spore print — check any tawny Cortinarius
Orellanine toxin: delayed symptoms mean no immediate warning
confusionWarnings: DEADLY — orellanine causes kidney failure; symptoms delayed 2–3 weeks after eating
Introduces an entire dangerous genus: all tawny-brown Cortinarius species should be treated with caution
No effective antidote; cases have required kidney transplants
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario conifer forest under spruce; not abundant but found each season in suitable habitat

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario spruce forest. Delayed kidney-destroying toxin makes this a critical safety species. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); North American Mycological Association.

descriptionShort: A spectacular deep violet Cortinarius — cap, gills, stem, and flesh all deep blue-violet. One of the most beautiful large mushrooms in Ontario forest.
descriptionLong: Violet Webcap is one of the most visually stunning fungi in Ontario: the entire mushroom — cap, stem, gills, and flesh — is deep, rich violet-blue. The cap is dry and fibrous or slightly scaly. The gills are dark violet, turning rusty brown as the spores mature. As in all Cortinarius, a cortina (cobweb veil) is present when young, connecting cap edge to stem, later leaving rusty-brown remnants on the stem. It grows from soil in mixed and deciduous forest, usually under beech or birch. A genuinely rare and exciting find. Not edible — all large Cortinarius are best avoided.

Cobweb cortina veil when young — rusty fibres on stem as it matures
Gills darken to rusty-brown with age
Large and dramatic — cap 8–15 cm

Cortina is present in young specimens — look for the silky veil connecting cap edge to stem
Rusty-brown spore print on the stem base confirms Cortinarius
confusionWarnings: Not edible — avoid eating all large Cortinarius species
Blewit (Lepista nuda) is also purple but has no cortina, pink spore print, and grows in leaf litter
encounterRarity: Uncommon to rare in suitable Ontario deciduous forest — a red-letter find when encountered

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Present but uncommon in Ontario mixed forest. All-violet colouration is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A small white mushroom with concentric brown scales and a rubbery-plastic smell. Toxic — introduces the Lepiota genus, which includes some deadly species.
descriptionLong: Dapperling is a small to medium mushroom with a white cap covered in concentric rings of reddish-brown scales, a smooth white stem with a fragile ring, and free white gills. The most distinctive feature is the smell: strongly chemical — like rubber, burning plastic, or coal tar. This smell is consistent and off-putting. It grows on rich soil, compost heaps, wood chip beds, and garden borders. Toxic — contains amatoxins in some specimens, though levels vary. The Lepiota genus includes species ranging from mildly to fatally toxic; all small Lepiota species should be treated with caution.
keyTraits: White cap with concentric reddish-brown scales
Strong rubbery, plastic, or coal tar smell
Fragile ring on smooth white stem
Free white gills
expertClues: Smell is the primary identifier — chemical and unpleasant
Smaller than Shaggy Parasol — rarely exceeds 6 cm cap
Brown scales arranged in concentric rings from the centre outward

All small white-capped Lepiota species in Ontario should be treated as potentially deadly
Do not confuse with edible Agaricus — Lepiota has free gills; Agaricus gills are pink to brown
encounterRarity: Regular in Ontario gardens, parks, and wood chip beds from summer through fall

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario gardens and parks. Rubber/plastic smell is the key field identifier. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); North American Mycological Association.

descriptionShort: A pale brown-capped bolete with a white stem covered in dark rough scales. Found exclusively under birch — one of the most common boletes in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Birch Bolete is one of Ontario's most common boletes, found under birch throughout the province. The cap is pale grey-brown to buff-brown, smooth, convex. The stem is white to pale grey with distinctive dark brown to black scabers — small rough scales or fibrils — scattered over the entire surface. This scabered stem is the key Leccinum genus feature. The flesh is white and does not stain blue when cut, though it slowly turns grey-pink then black with age. Pores are white to grey. Found strictly under birch. Edible but requires thorough cooking; raw consumption can cause illness.
keyTraits: Pale grey-brown to buff cap
White stem covered in dark brown-black scabers (rough scales)
Flesh slowly darkens to grey-pink then black — does not blue
Strictly under birch
expertClues: Scabers on stem are the key Leccinum genus feature — present in all Leccinum species
Habitat under birch is diagnostic for L. scabrum
White pores; no ring; no volva
confusionWarnings: Requires thorough cooking — raw consumption causes illness
Orange Birch Bolete (Leccinum versipelle) has an orange cap; both are under birch
encounterRarity: One of the most common boletes in Ontario; found under birch throughout the province

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Very common under Ontario birch. Introduces the Leccinum (scaber-stemmed bolete) genus. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A striking bolete with a vivid orange cap and a white stem covered in dark scabers. Found under birch and aspen — the most colourful of the Ontario Leccinum.
descriptionLong: Orange Birch Bolete is the most visually striking of the Ontario Leccinum species: the cap is vivid orange to orange-red — a sharp contrast to the pale cap of Birch Bolete. The stem is white with distinctive dark scabers. The pores are white to grey. The flesh is white but turns pink to dark grey then almost black when cut — an interesting transformation. It grows under birch and aspen, forming mycorrhizal associations with both. Like Birch Bolete, it requires thorough cooking. The orange cap against a birch or aspen trunk backdrop is one of Ontario's more photogenic bolete scenes.
keyTraits: Vivid orange to orange-red cap
White stem with dark brown-black scabers
Flesh turns pink then dark grey-black when cut
Under birch and aspen
expertClues: Orange cap is unmistakeable among Leccinum — no other scaber-stemmed bolete in Ontario is this colour
Flesh darkening is dramatic — watch for the colour change over 15–20 minutes

confusionWarnings: Requires thorough cooking — raw consumption causes illness

encounterRarity: Present in Ontario birch and aspen forest; less common than Birch Bolete but a rewarding find

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario birch and aspen forest. Orange cap is distinctive among Leccinum. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A bolete that looks almost exactly like King Bolete — but the pores are pink and the taste is intensely, incredibly bitter. Not toxic, but ruins any dish it contaminates.
descriptionLong: Bitter Bolete is one of the most important boletes for Ontario foragers to know because it mimics the King Bolete (porcini) almost perfectly. The cap is pale brown to tan, convex. The stem has a reticulate net pattern. But two features separate it: the pores are pink to rose (not white-cream), and the taste is shockingly, persistently bitter — even a tiny piece will ruin a dish. It grows from soil in mixed and conifer forest. Not toxic, but a single specimen mixed with King Boletes will make an entire meal inedible. The pink pore and bitter taste tests are essential knowledge for any bolete forager.
keyTraits: Pale brown to tan cap — very similar to King Bolete
Pores are pink to rose — NOT white or cream
Intensely bitter taste — persistent and unmistakeable
Net pattern on stem
expertClues: Pink pores are diagnostic — check immediately before harvesting any King Bolete
Tiny taste test (spit immediately): overwhelming bitterness confirms identity
Does not blue when cut — flesh stays white

Nearly identical to King Bolete in appearance — always check pore colour and taste
encounterRarity: Very common in Ontario forest — arguably more abundant than King Bolete in many areas

images.attribution: (c) Federico Calledda, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Federico Calledda

sourceNotes: Very common in Ontario. The pink pore and taste test are essential bolete foraging skills. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A blood-red to liver-coloured tongue-shaped bracket that bleeds red juice when cut. Grows on old oaks. Looks remarkably like a slab of raw beef.
descriptionLong: Beefsteak Fungus is one of Ontario's most visually dramatic brackets. It produces a single thick, tongue or fan-shaped fruiting body with a vivid blood-red to liver-brown surface. The upper surface is moist and sticky, sometimes resembling a raw tongue. The pore surface underneath is cream-coloured to pale yellow, with individual tubes that are easily separated. Most remarkably, when cut the flesh is streaked dark red and pale — resembling raw beef — and exudes a red juice. It has a sour, acidic taste. Grows on old oaks and sweet chestnuts. Stains the wood of living oaks, producing the prized 'brown oak' valued in woodworking.

Exudes red juice when cut; streaked red-and-pale flesh
Cream to yellow pores that separate individually
On old oak — usually at the base of large trees

Edible when young but sour taste is strong; best used sparingly
encounterRarity: Present on old oaks in Ontario parks and woodland; not common but consistent where large oaks grow

sourceNotes: Present on large old oaks in southern Ontario. Red bleeding flesh is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: The classic horse-hoof shaped conk on birch and beech. Used by humans for fire-starting for thousands of years — found with Ötzi the Iceman. Year-round.
descriptionLong: Tinder Polypore is one of the most iconic bracket fungi in the world. It grows as a perennial, hard, hoof-shaped bracket with a grey to grey-brown zoned surface, adding new layers of growth each year. The rim is rounded and the bracket can grow very large over decades. The pore surface is cream-brown and bruises darker when pressed. The flesh is hard and corky, with a distinctive tough fibrous inner layer — the amadou — which was used for centuries to catch and carry sparks for fire-starting. Found year-round on birch and beech across Ontario. The find associated with Ötzi the Iceman (3300 BCE) was Fomes fomentarius.

Zoned, hard surface — adds a new layer each year
Tough corky fibrous flesh — the amadou layer
On birch and beech — year-round

sourceNotes: Year-round on Ontario birch and beech. Historical use for fire-starting. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: A hard, charcoal-black, irregular mass on birch trunks — looks like burnt wood. Not a true fruiting body, but one of the most sought-after fungi in the world.
descriptionLong: Chaga is unlike anything else in the Ontario forest. It appears as a large, hard, irregular mass on birch trunks — jet black, cracked, and lumpy on the outside, looking exactly like a charred knot of wood. The interior is golden-orange to rust-brown and hard. This is the sterile conk — the visible stage most people know. The true fruiting body forms under the bark of dying birch and is rarely seen. Chaga is a pathogen of birch but has attracted enormous interest worldwide for medicinal compounds. It grows only on living or recently dead birch. It is present across northern and central Ontario wherever mature birch grows.

Black burnt exterior; golden-orange interior
Only on birch
Year-round — the conk persists for years

encounterRarity: Present on mature birch across Ontario; more common in older forest; a notable find

descriptionShort: Masses of small white to tan pear-shaped puffballs growing directly from rotten logs and stumps. The only puffball in Ontario that grows on wood.
descriptionLong: Pear-shaped Puffball is very common and distinctive because it grows on wood — rotten logs, stumps, and buried wood debris — rather than from soil like most puffballs. Fruiting bodies are small, pear-shaped or top-shaped, white to tan, with a short sterile base attached to the wood. The surface is covered in tiny spines or granules that wear off, leaving a smooth or slightly roughened skin. Like all puffballs, the interior must be pure white for edibility. They grow in dense clusters, often covering an entire stump. The only Ontario puffball on wood.

White to tan; grows in dense clusters on rotten stumps
Short sterile base attaches to wood
Only Ontario puffball on wood — substrate is diagnostic

descriptionShort: A distinctive vase-shaped fungus with a tan to ochre top and a wrinkled violet-tan underside. Grows in clusters under conifers. A genuine special find.
descriptionLong: Pig's Ear is a distinctive and beautiful fungus that resembles a chunky chanterelle or a flattened vase. The top surface is tan to ochre-brown and smooth. The underside has irregular, forking, blunt ridges or wrinkles in shades of lilac to tan. The flesh is pale and firm. It grows from soil in clusters under conifers — particularly spruce and fir — in cool, moist forest. Despite its chanterelle-like appearance, it belongs to a different family. Present in Ontario but not common; finding a cluster is a memorable occasion. Considered edible by some authorities.

Under conifer — especially spruce; not usually with hardwoods

sourceNotes: Present but uncommon in Ontario conifer forest. Violet ridges on underside are distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo); Ontario Mycological Society.

descriptionShort: Vivid amethyst-violet upright clubs in moss and grassland. One of the most startlingly colourful fungi in Ontario — and an indicator of old-growth habitat.
descriptionLong: Purple Fairy Club is one of the most visually arresting fungi in Ontario: clusters of vivid violet to amethyst-purple upright clubs, 3–8 cm tall, growing from moss or soil in old woodland, unimproved grassland, or mossy forest clearings. The clubs are fragile and waxy. The colour is intense when fresh, fading slightly with age. Like waxcap mushrooms, Clavaria species are considered indicators of ancient, undisturbed grassland and woodland habitat — finding them suggests the ground has not been ploughed or heavily managed for decades. Not edible but unmistakeable and significant.

Fragile and waxy

descriptionShort: A tiny, rubbery, club-shaped fungus with a gelatinous yellow-green head on a pale stem. Grows in crowds on forest soil. Odd, charming, and hard to classify.
descriptionLong: Jelly Baby is a small but distinctive ascomycete that looks like a tiny club or lollipop: a short, pale yellow to ochre stem topped with a rounded, irregular, gelatinous head in yellow-green or olive-green tones. The head is viscid (slightly sticky) and its surface is the spore-producing zone. The stem is sometimes darker-flecked or pale orange. They grow in clusters on moist forest soil, especially in mossy areas and beside streams, in deciduous and mixed forest. Their strange jelly-like texture makes them one of the more unusual fungi for young naturalists to encounter. Not edible.

Pale ochre to yellow stem

Head is irregular and lobed — not a perfect ball
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — the gelatinous head on a pale stem is unique in Ontario
Not to be confused with Leotia atrovirens, which is entirely dark green
encounterRarity: Common in moist Ontario forest in summer and fall; easy to overlook due to small size

sourceNotes: Common in moist Ontario forest. Gelatinous head on pale stem is distinctive. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: Tiny cup fungi that look exactly like miniature bird's nests complete with 'eggs' (spore packets). One of the most extraordinary small fungi in Ontario.
descriptionLong: Bird's Nest Fungus is one of the most extraordinary fungi in Ontario, tiny in size but remarkable in design. Each fruiting body is a small cup, 0.5–1.5 cm across, shaped like a flared nest with striated (fluted) walls of grey-brown. Inside the cup sit several small disc-shaped spore packets called peridioles — the 'eggs.' When a raindrop falls into the cup, the splash propels the peridioles outward, dispersing spores. The peridioles are attached by a coiled string (funicular cord) that can wrap around plant stems. Found on wood chips, mulch, dead sticks, and plant debris. Impossible to mistake once seen.
keyTraits: Tiny cup or nest — 0.5–1.5 cm
Fluted or striated inner cup walls
Multiple cream to grey disc-shaped 'eggs' inside
On wood chips and woody debris
expertClues: Striated (grooved) inner walls are the key feature of Cyathus striatus
Eggs (peridioles) are attached to the cup by a coiled funicular cord
Often in clusters — dozens of nests together on a patch of mulch
confusionWarnings: No dangerous lookalikes — appearance is completely unique
Crucibulum laeve is a similar bird's nest with smooth inner cup walls and cream-coloured eggs
encounterRarity: Common on wood chips and woody debris across Ontario; easy to overlook due to tiny size

sourceNotes: Common on Ontario wood chips and mulch. Splash-cup spore dispersal is extraordinary. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A dark grey-black saddle-shaped cap on a white ribbed-and-channelled stem. Looks like a tiny gothic architectural detail dropped on the forest floor.
descriptionLong: Elf Saddle is one of the most architecturally distinctive fungi in Ontario. The cap is grey to dark grey-black, saddle-shaped to irregularly lobed, with a surface that is smooth to wrinkled on the upper (spore-producing) side. The stem is the most striking feature: white to pale grey, hollow, and deeply fluted and ridged with an irregular network of channels and grooves. The whole effect is of something sculpted rather than grown. Found on soil in mixed and conifer forest, often along paths. Not edible and contains traces of gyromitrin. A superb observation find.
keyTraits: Dark grey to black saddle-shaped or lobed cap
White stem with deep irregular flutes and channels
Hollow throughout
From soil in forest
expertClues: Stem fluting is the most distinctive feature — deeply ridged and channelled
Cap is free from the stem at the margins — not fused down the sides like a morel
Spring and fall — two fruiting windows
confusionWarnings: Contains traces of gyromitrin — do not eat
Distinguished from False Morel (Gyromitra) by saddle or lobe shape rather than brain-like mass, and by the deeply fluted stem
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario forest in spring and fall; encountered regularly on forest paths

sourceNotes: Present in Ontario mixed and conifer forest in spring and fall. Fluted stem is unmistakeable. Reference: MushroomExpert.com (Kuo).

descriptionShort: A deep, dark brown-black urn or goblet-shaped cup fungus in early spring. One of the first fungi of the year — appears before leaves open.
descriptionLong: Devil's Urn is a striking early spring fungus: a deep, goblet or urn-shaped cup up to 6 cm across, dark grey-brown to near-black inside, with a pale, finely hairy outer surface. The cup is deep and narrow at first, opening wider as it matures. Young cups have a small, ragged-edged opening that widens. They grow on buried sticks and woody debris of hardwoods, often beech, in moist forest, usually along streams or in sheltered hollows. They appear in early spring before most other fungi — March to May in Ontario — making them a distinctive sign that the fungal season is beginning again. Not edible but dramatic.
keyTraits: Deep, dark brown-black urn or goblet-shaped cup
Pale hairy outer surface; dark smooth interior
Very early spring — before leaf-out
On buried hardwood sticks in moist forest
expertClues: Early season timing is the best clue — March to May, with Scarlet Cups and before morels
Deep, narrow urn shape distinguishes it from shallower cup fungi
Often found in groups along stream banks under beech and maple
confusionWarnings: Scarlet Cup is red and shallower — different colour and shape
No dangerous lookalikes — appearance is distinctive
encounterRarity: Present in Ontario moist hardwood forest in early spring; a rewarding early-season find
